proposals.title: Decomposing Auditory Processing in Hearing-Impaired Listeners: A Diffusion Decision Model Analysis of Speech Identification Response Times
proposals.motivation: Current clinical speech tests primarily measure accuracy, providing an incomplete picture of the listening effort and underlying cognitive processes involved, especially for individuals with hearing loss or tinnitus. Pre-2026 research in cognitive psychology has successfully used diffusion models to decompose response time (RT) data from decision tasks into distinct components like information processing speed and response caution, but this powerful technique has seen limited application in auditory psychophysics, particularly for complex signals like speech.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fitting the Diffusion Decision Model (DDM) to RT distributions from speech-in-noise identification tasks will reveal that hearing loss and tinnitus primarily affect the rate of evidence accumulation (drift rate), reflecting degraded peripheral input, while cognitive factors like listening effort and attention will be reflected in changes to the decision boundary separation.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing and new datasets from speech identification tests (e.g., words in modulated noise) collected from groups with normal hearing, sensorineural hearing loss, and tinnitus. For each participant and condition, we will fit the hierarchical Bayesian Diffusion Decision Model (HDDM) to the joint distribution of correct/error responses and their RTs. The model parameters—drift rate (v), boundary separation (a), and non-decision time (t0)—will be estimated. We will then use linear mixed-effects models to test how these parameters are influenced by audiological factors (e.g., degree of high-frequency loss), stimulus factors (e.g., SNR), and group status (tinnitus vs. control).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework for separating sensory degradation from cognitive and strategic factors in auditory speech perception. It will offer new, model-based biomarkers for listening effort and processing efficiency that go beyond accuracy scores. The findings could inform the development of more targeted hearing aid signal processing strategies that aim to optimize the rate of usable evidence delivery.
proposals.novelty: While DDMs are established in visual decision neuroscience, their application to decompose RTs in ecologically valid auditory speech tasks is novel. Combining this sophisticated cognitive modeling approach with Professor Moore's deep expertise in individual differences in auditory processing and hearing loss creates a unique and powerful synergy.

proposals.title: Beyond Loudness: Quantifying the Relative Contributions of Tonality, Roughness, and Sharpness to Subjective Annoyance in Complex Sounds
proposals.motivation: Current noise assessment and hearing-aid processing heavily rely on loudness-based metrics (e.g., A-weighting, NAL-R/NL2) to predict annoyance, a key factor in hearing-aid acceptance and environmental noise policy. However, clinical and anecdotal evidence suggests sounds of equal loudness can cause vastly different annoyance levels, indicating other psychoacoustic qualities are critical. A systematic, model-driven investigation is needed to move beyond the loudness-centric paradigm and improve predictive models of sound tolerance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for spectro-temporally complex sounds, including those with energy in frequency regions typical of hearing loss, psychoacoustic metrics of tonality, roughness, and sharpness will be stronger predictors of subjective annoyance than loudness alone.
proposals.method: We will generate a diverse set of complex stimuli (e.g., modulated noises, harmonic complexes, real-world environmental sounds) with controlled variations in tonality, roughness, sharpness, and overall level. Annoyance judgments will be collected from groups of listeners with normal hearing and sensorineural hearing loss using magnitude estimation. For each group, we will employ a Deep Neural Network (DNN) regression model, using pre-2026 architectures like a multi-layer perceptron or a convolutional neural network on spectro-temporal representations, to predict annoyance ratings from a feature vector containing Zwicker loudness, tonality (based on prominence ratio), roughness (based on modulation depth and frequency), and sharpness. The relative importance of each feature will be determined via permutation feature importance and SHAP analysis on the trained DNN.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, data-driven model of auditory annoyance that disentangles the contributions of key sound qualities, challenging the primacy of loudness. It will offer a new framework for diagnosing individual noise tolerance profiles in clinical audiology and inform the next generation of hearing-aid sound-processing algorithms that aim to reduce annoyance, not just loudness.
proposals.novelty: While individual psychoacoustic metrics are well-studied, this proposal uniquely applies modern DNN-based analysis to a controlled perceptual dataset to directly compare and rank their predictive power for annoyance across hearing-status groups, moving from correlation to causal modeling of this complex percept.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Perceptual Valence of Time-Varying Sound Patterns: From Annoyance to Neutrality or Preference
proposals.motivation: Auditory research, particularly in hearing loss, tinnitus, and hearing aid design, predominantly focuses on noise as a detrimental factor that masks signals, causes annoyance, or exacerbates conditions like tinnitus. This negative framing overlooks the possibility that certain complex, non-speech, time-varying sounds may be perceived as neutral or even positive, which could inform new sound therapy strategies or improve soundscape design in assistive devices. A systematic investigation is needed to move beyond the assumption that all non-informative sound is perceptually negative.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific acoustic features—such as modulated frequency contours, spectral-temporal complexity, and harmonic relationships—can shift the perceptual valence of a sound from annoying to neutral or preferred, even for listeners with hearing loss or tinnitus.
proposals.method: We will generate controlled, time-varying sound patterns (e.g., frequency-modulated tones, filtered noise with temporal gaps, and synthetic textures) using a psychoacoustic synthesis toolbox. A deep neural network (DNN) architecture, similar to those used for diagnosing noise-induced hearing loss, will be trained to predict subjective valence ratings (annoying/neutral/pleasant) from acoustic features. The study will involve participants with normal hearing and varying degrees of sensorineural hearing loss, assessing perception using adaptive psychophysical procedures for discrimination and categorical rating, while also measuring tinnitus distress via standardized questionnaires.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a foundational framework for classifying sounds by perceptual valence rather than just acoustic type, directly challenging the monolithic view of noise. It could lead to novel auditory stimuli for therapeutic sound enrichment, offering alternatives to traditional masking noise for tinnitus management. The findings will also inform hearing aid algorithms, suggesting parameters for 'positive' or neutral environmental sound preservation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the core assumption that non-speech, non-musical sound is inherently negative, systematically seeking acoustic correlates of neutral or positive perception. Integrating DNN-based feature analysis with classic psychoacoustic methods from frequency and temporal processing research to address perceptual valence is a new synthesis in auditory science.

proposals.title: Individualized Auditory Annoyance Modelling: Integrating Audiometric Phenotypes, Genetic Markers, and Speech-in-Noise Performance
proposals.motivation: Current models of noise-induced annoyance and hearing loss often assume a universal response, yet clinical and military data show vast individual variability in susceptibility. This 'one-size-fits-all' approach limits the accuracy of diagnostic tools and the personalization of hearing protection and rehabilitation strategies. A model that accounts for individual differences in auditory processing and genetic predisposition is needed to move towards precision audiology.
proposals.hypothesis: Individual susceptibility to auditory annoyance and noise-induced hearing loss can be robustly predicted by a multivariate model integrating extended high-frequency audiometry, speech-in-noise temporal processing deficits, and a polygenic risk score derived from known candidate genes (e.g., GJB2, KCNQ4, GRM7).
proposals.method: We will recruit a cohort of 300 adults with varied noise exposure histories (military veterans and civilians). Phenotypic data will include detailed audiometry (0.125–16 kHz), temporal modulation detection, and speech-in-noise tests (e.g., QuickSIN). Deep Neural Networks (DNNs), similar to those Moore's group used for NIHL diagnosis, will be trained to predict individual annoyance ratings (from laboratory noise exposures) and audiological outcomes. Genetic data will be analyzed for a panel of pre-2026 candidate genes associated with hearing, and a polygenic risk score will be incorporated as a feature in the DNN models to assess its predictive power beyond phenotypic measures alone.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, individualized framework for assessing auditory risk, moving beyond population averages. It will directly inform the development of personalized hearing conservation programs and provide a methodological blueprint for integrating genetic and phenotypic data in auditory models. The resulting DNN model could be adapted for clinical screening tools to identify high-risk individuals proactively.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has modeled individual differences in frequency discrimination or diagnosed NIHL, this proposal uniquely integrates genetic predisposition with extended high-frequency and temporal speech-in-noise phenotypes within a single predictive DNN framework to model the continuum from subtle annoyance to overt pathology.

proposals.title: Real-time, Individualized Prediction of Noise Annoyance Using a Wearable Acoustic Monitor and Context-Aware Deep Learning
proposals.motivation: Noise annoyance is a significant public health issue linked to stress and reduced well-being, but it is highly subjective and influenced by both acoustic features and personal context. Current environmental noise monitoring focuses on average sound levels (e.g., Leq), failing to capture the individual perceptual experience that determines annoyance. A system that can estimate personal annoyance in real-time would revolutionize noise exposure assessment and personalized hearing health interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: A deep neural network model, trained on synchronized acoustic data, individual hearing profiles (including frequency-specific loss), and contextual self-reports, can accurately predict moment-to-moment subjective annoyance ratings in real-world environments.
proposals.method: We will develop a smartphone-linked wearable device (e.g., a research hearing aid or earpiece) to capture high-fidelity acoustic data. A pre-2026 deep learning architecture (e.g., a convolutional-recurrent neural network) will extract multi-dimensional acoustic features (spectro-temporal, loudness, sharpness, roughness). These will be combined with individual audiometric data (simulating 'deep marginal feedback cancellation' methods for personalization) and contextual tags (location, activity) input by the user. The model will be trained and validated using ecological momentary assessment, where participants provide real-time annoyance ratings via the smartphone app over several weeks.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first validated system for real-time, personalized noise annoyance monitoring, moving beyond population-level metrics. It will provide a novel tool for epidemiological studies on noise and health and lay the groundwork for 'smart' hearing devices that can actively manage soundscapes to reduce wearer annoyance. The dataset will be a unique resource for understanding the interaction between acoustic cues, hearing loss, and context in auditory perception.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has modeled annoyance from laboratory sounds, this proposal integrates real-world acoustic streaming, individual hearing profiles (exploiting Moore's work on frequency analysis and loss), and contextual data within a real-time deep learning framework—a significant step towards truly personalized auditory scene management.

proposals.title: Inverse Psychoacoustic Optimization: A Deep Learning Framework for Designing Minimally Annoying Product Sounds
proposals.motivation: The sound signatures of consumer products and vehicles are critical for user experience and regulatory compliance, yet sound design often relies on trial-and-error. Pre-2026 annoyance models, like those based on loudness, sharpness, and fluctuation strength, can predict annoyance but not generate optimal sounds. An inverse solution is needed to engineer sounds that are perceptually less intrusive from the outset, benefiting both hearing-impaired and normal-hearing individuals in noisy environments.
proposals.hypothesis: A deep neural network, trained on pre-2026 psychoacoustic annoyance metrics and auditory models, can be inverted to generate time-frequency sound profiles that minimize predicted annoyance while meeting engineering constraints on source output.
proposals.method: We will develop a conditional Generative Adversarial Network (cGAN) architecture. The generator will take a target source spectrum (e.g., from a household appliance) and a constraint vector (e.g., overall level limits) as input, outputting a modified time-frequency profile. The discriminator will be trained jointly on real sounds and a loss function combining adversarial loss, a fidelity term, and a psychoacoustic loss computed using a pre-validated annoyance model (e.g., Zwicker's model). We will validate generated sounds using perceptual tests with normal-hearing and hearing-impaired listeners, the latter informed by Moore's work on frequency resolution and loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first generalizable, data-driven framework for the inverse design of less annoying sounds, moving beyond descriptive models to prescriptive engineering. It will demonstrate how auditory models and deep learning can be combined to solve a practical industrial acoustics problem, with direct applications in hearing aid feedback cancellation and noise management.
proposals.novelty: While deep networks have been used for sound classification and enhancement, their application to *inverse* psychoacoustic optimization for annoyance minimization is novel. The approach explicitly bridges high-fidelity auditory modeling (Moore's expertise) with modern generative AI to create a new design tool.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Auditory and Cognitive Restoration from Low-Annoyance Soundscapes: A Model-Based Approach Using Individualized Hearing Profiles
proposals.motivation: Auditory research, particularly in hearing loss and tinnitus, has predominantly focused on the detrimental effects of noise and the restoration of speech intelligibility. However, the positive, restorative benefits of quiet, low-annoyance acoustic environments remain poorly quantified. This is a critical gap, as understanding these benefits could inform therapeutic soundscape design for tinnitus relief, stress reduction, and cognitive recovery in individuals with and without hearing loss.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that exposure to tailored, low-annoyance soundscapes will lead to measurable, model-predictable improvements in auditory processing stability (e.g., reduced temporal and frequency discrimination variability) and cognitive load, with the magnitude of benefit being predictable from an individual's audiometric profile and central auditory processing metrics.
proposals.method: We will recruit groups with normal hearing, sensorineural hearing loss, and chronic tinnitus. Participants will undergo baseline psychoacoustic tests (frequency discrimination at 2 kHz, temporal modulation detection) and cognitive tasks. A deep neural network (DNN) architecture, pre-trained on auditory scene analysis and adapted from our prior feedback cancellation work, will be used to generate personalized, low-annoyance soundscapes by minimizing predicted annoyance features. The model's core will integrate individualized hearing thresholds and loudness growth data. Pre- and post-soundscape exposure changes in psychoacoustic and cognitive performance will be measured and modeled as a function of the DNN's soundscape parameters and participant profile.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first computational model linking individualized auditory profiles to quantifiable restorative benefits of soundscapes, moving beyond qualitative assessments. It will establish a methodological framework for prescribing 'acoustic rest' environments as a complement to existing audiological rehabilitation strategies, particularly for tinnitus management.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a model-driven, DNN-based approach—typically used for diagnosing hearing loss or enhancing speech—to the inverse problem of engineering restorative quiet, and in defining the benefit via objective psychoacoustic and cognitive metrics rather than subjective ratings alone.

proposals.title: Blind Source Separation for Hearing Aids: A CASA-Informed Deep Learning Approach to Improve Speech Intelligibility in Complex Noise
proposals.motivation: A primary complaint of hearing aid users is poor speech understanding in noisy, multi-talker environments, as conventional directional microphones and noise reduction struggle with non-stationary, spectrally overlapping interferers. Computational Auditory Scene Analysis (CASA) principles offer a biologically inspired framework for source separation, but their real-time application in hearing aids remains a significant challenge. This project addresses a critical bottleneck in hearing aid processing by integrating CASA cues with modern blind source separation techniques.
proposals.hypothesis: A deep neural network (DNN) architecture, explicitly trained to leverage CASA-inspired auditory grouping cues (e.g., common onset/offset, harmonicity, spatial location), will outperform conventional DNN-based noise reduction in separating target speech from competing talkers, leading to measurable improvements in speech reception thresholds for listeners with hearing loss.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-channel DNN model that takes binaural microphone signals from a hearing aid form factor as input. The network architecture will incorporate intermediate loss functions designed to estimate and enhance classic CASA features (e.g., a time-frequency mask based on pitch and spatial cues derived from interaural phase differences). This model will be trained and evaluated using established databases of noisy speech mixtures (e.g., CHiME, WSJ0). Performance will be assessed using objective metrics (e.g., SI-SDR, PESQ) and via a listening study with hearing-impaired participants, comparing it to a state-of-the-art deep marginal feedback cancellation system as a baseline.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, principled integration of CASA theory with data-driven DNNs for hearing aids, moving beyond black-box approaches. It will yield a validated processing algorithm that demonstrably improves signal-to-noise ratio for speech in complex acoustic scenes and a framework for interpreting network decisions through the lens of auditory perceptual organization.
proposals.novelty: While DNNs have been applied to speech separation, their explicit architectural constraint and training to emulate known auditory grouping principles for a hearing aid application is novel. This bridges the gap between classical psychoacoustic/CASA models and modern machine learning, offering interpretability and a stronger foundation in auditory science.

proposals.title: Spectral Dependence of Loudness Summation: A Test of the Frequency-Independence Assumption Using Complex Sounds
proposals.motivation: Classical loudness models often assume that loudness summation across frequency bands is independent of spectral content, a simplification that may limit their accuracy for real-world sounds like speech and music. This assumption is particularly critical for hearing aid fitting and noise-induced hearing loss assessment, where accurate loudness perception is key. Testing this foundational assumption with modern stimuli and participant groups, including those with hearing loss, is a necessary step for model refinement.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that loudness summation across frequency bands is not frequency-independent but is systematically influenced by the spectral density and bandwidth of the stimulus, with effects that differ between normal-hearing listeners and those with sensorineural hearing loss.
proposals.method: We will conduct psychoacoustic experiments using normal-hearing and hearing-impaired participants. Stimuli will include narrowband noises, harmonic complexes, and speech-shaped noises of varying bandwidths and spectral centroids. Loudness matches will be obtained using an adaptive two-interval forced-choice procedure, comparing the test sound to a reference tone. Data analysis will employ mixed-effects modeling to quantify the influence of spectral variables on summation exponents, drawing on Moore's recent work on individual differences in frequency discrimination.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct empirical test of a core assumption in loudness modeling, potentially leading to more accurate predictions for complex sounds. The findings will inform the development of next-generation hearing aid compression and feedback cancellation algorithms (extending Moore's recent DNN work) by offering a refined perceptual basis for loudness normalization.
proposals.novelty: While loudness summation has been studied for broadband noise, the systematic investigation of how summation varies with specific spectral profiles and in hearing-impaired listeners inverts the standard modeling assumption. This approach integrates Moore's expertise in individual differences and frequency processing with applied hearing aid research.

proposals.title: System Identification of the Middle-Ear Transfer Function: A Novel Approach Using Wideband Reflectance and Control-Theoretic Models
proposals.motivation: Accurate characterization of the middle-ear transfer function (METF) is crucial for diagnosing conductive pathologies, refining auditory models, and improving hearing-aid fitting algorithms. Pre-2026 methods, such as laser Doppler vibrometry or derived estimates from reflectance, are often complex, invasive, or rely on simplified assumptions. A robust, direct, and physiologically grounded system identification method is needed to capture individual variability, especially in cases of subtle or noise-induced conductive component.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that control-theoretic system identification techniques, applied to non-invasive wideband acoustic reflectance measurements and simultaneous intra-tympanic pressure data, can robustly estimate a linear time-invariant (LTI) model of the METF that outperforms existing phenomenological models in predictive accuracy across frequency.
proposals.method: We will collect wideband reflectance and phase data alongside intra-tympanic pressure (as the system 'input') and cochlear microphonic or derived stapes velocity estimates (as the 'output') in an animal model (e.g., guinea pig). Using pre-2026 system identification toolboxes (MATLAB's System Identification Toolbox), we will apply subspace identification methods (N4SID) and prediction-error minimization (PEM) to fit discrete-time state-space and autoregressive with exogenous input (ARX) models. Model order will be selected using Akaike's Information Criterion (AIC), and validation will be performed on a withheld dataset using time- and frequency-domain error metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mathematically rigorous framework for deriving individualized METFs, directly linking measurable inputs to physiological outputs. It will contribute a set of validated, parsimonious models that can be integrated into existing auditory perception models (like those used by Moore's group for frequency and speech research) to better separate middle-ear from cochlear contributions in hearing loss profiles.
proposals.novelty: While system identification is standard in engineering, its direct application to derive the METF from paired physiological input-output measurements is novel. This approach moves beyond descriptive curve-fitting to a causal, dynamical systems framework, explicitly treating the middle ear as a 'black-box' system to be identified.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating the Role of Temporal Envelope Cues in Speech Intelligibility for Listeners with Cochlear Dead Regions
proposals.motivation: A dominant pre-2026 assumption in auditory science is that sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL), particularly with cochlear dead regions, degrades the fidelity of temporal envelope cues, contributing to poor speech perception. This drives complex hearing-aid algorithms aiming to restore fine structure or spectral detail. However, evidence that envelope cues alone can support high speech intelligibility in normal hearing challenges this assumption, suggesting a need for a direct test in impaired hearing.
proposals.hypothesis: For listeners with moderate-to-severe SNHL and diagnosed cochlear dead regions, speech intelligibility for sentences in noise will not be significantly degraded when listening to signals preserving only temporal envelope information (via channel vocoding) compared to listening to the original, full-spectrum speech, provided the envelope extraction is optimized for the individual's residual hearing.
proposals.method: We will recruit a group of listeners with SNHL and audiometrically diagnosed dead regions (using the Threshold Equalizing Noise test). Speech reception thresholds (SRTs) will be measured for IEEE sentences in speech-shaped noise. Stimuli will be processed through a 16-channel noise-excited vocoder, a pre-2026 standard for envelope extraction. The novel step is to individually tailor the channel analysis filters to avoid dead regions, mapping information exclusively to regions of residual function. Performance with this 'tailored envelope' will be compared to unprocessed speech and to a standard vocoder with fixed, place-based filters.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test a core assumption about perceptual deficits in SNHL, potentially demonstrating that accessible envelope cues are more robust than believed. It could shift focus in hearing-aid design from attempting to restore lost fine structure to more effectively enhancing and delivering envelope information tailored to the individual's cochlear map.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the classic degradation hypothesis and testing it with a rigorous, individualized method that explicitly accounts for dead regions, moving beyond group-average data to exploit individual differences in cochlear pathology.

proposals.title: Audiometric Frequency Dependence of Temporal Fine Structure (TFS) Processing in Normal-Hearing and Hearing-Impaired Listeners
proposals.motivation: The role of Temporal Fine Structure (TFS) processing in auditory perception, particularly for pitch, binaural hearing, and speech-in-noise, is a central topic in hearing science. Pre-2026 research, including Moore's own work, often suggests TFS sensitivity is reduced in hearing loss, but it is unclear if this deficit is uniform across the audiometric range or varies with frequency, which has critical implications for hearing-aid signal processing strategies and models of cochlear damage.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the ability to use TFS cues is not globally reduced but exhibits significant frequency-dependent variation, with deficits potentially more pronounced in regions of hearing loss but not absent in regions of normal audiometric thresholds.
proposals.method: We will measure TFS sensitivity using established psychoacoustic tasks (e.g., interaural phase difference discrimination and monaural frequency modulation detection) at multiple frequencies (e.g., 500 Hz, 1 kHz, 2 kHz, 4 kHz) in groups of normal-hearing and hearing-impaired listeners with varying audiometric configurations. Deep neural network architectures, similar to those Moore has used for diagnosing noise-induced loss, will be employed to analyze the complex, high-dimensional relationship between individual audiometric profiles, etiologies (e.g., noise exposure history from military service data), and multi-frequency TFS performance, controlling for place and phase-locking cue confounds.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a detailed, frequency-specific map of TFS processing abilities, challenging the notion of a global deficit. It will directly inform the next generation of frequency-dependent hearing-aid algorithms, such as deep marginal feedback cancellers, by indicating where TFS preservation is most beneficial. The findings will refine physiological models of cochlear and neural dysfunction.
proposals.novelty: While TFS deficits are known, systematically testing their uniformity across frequencies in relation to individual audiometric and etiological profiles is novel. Combining rigorous psychoacoustics with a deep learning analytical framework to disentangle these individual differences represents a significant methodological advance beyond group-mean comparisons.

proposals.title: Enhanced Temporal Fine-Structure Processing in Musicians: Implications for Speech-in-Noise Perception and Tinnitus Mechanisms
proposals.motivation: Temporal fine-structure (TFS) processing is crucial for understanding speech in fluctuating noise and may be linked to tinnitus perception. Pre-2026 research by Moore and others shows large individual variability in TFS sensitivity, often measured via frequency discrimination at low frequencies or tasks like the TFS-LF test. Understanding the upper limits of this ability in populations like musicians can clarify the role of experience-dependent plasticity in auditory processing and inform models of hearing loss and tinnitus.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that trained musicians will exhibit superior behavioral sensitivity to TFS cues compared to non-musicians, and that this enhanced ability will correlate with better performance in complex speech-in-noise tasks and distinct psychoacoustic profiles related to tinnitus susceptibility.
proposals.method: We will recruit two age- and hearing-matched groups: professional musicians and non-musicians with normal hearing up to 8 kHz. TFS ability will be assessed using established pre-2026 psychoacoustic protocols: the TFS-LF test and a modified frequency discrimination task at 500 Hz and 2 kHz with randomized phase and level roving to isolate TFS cues. Speech perception will be evaluated using the University of Montreal's speech-in-noise test with modulated noise. Tinnitus-like percepts will be probed using notched-noise masking and pitch-matching tasks. Data analysis will employ linear mixed-effects models and, inspired by Moore's recent work, a deep neural network (DNN) classifier (e.g., a ResNet architecture) trained on individual psychoacoustic profiles to predict group membership and speech-in-noise scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a clear psychophysical benchmark for exceptional TFS processing, linking it to real-world auditory benefits. It will provide new evidence on the plasticity of the adult auditory system and offer insights into why individuals with similar audiograms may differ drastically in speech perception and tinnitus experiences, informing future hearing-aid processing strategies.
proposals.novelty: While musicians are known to have enhanced pitch perception, this study specifically targets the low-level TFS processing mechanisms, directly connecting them to speech-in-noise outcomes and tinnitus-related psychoacoustics using a combination of classic methods and modern DNN-based predictive modeling as a novel analytical tool.

proposals.title: Nonlinear Transmission in the Outer and Middle Ear at High Levels: A Combined Psychoacoustic and Deep Learning Analysis
proposals.motivation: Standard auditory models assume linear outer/middle ear (OME) filtering, which is crucial for accurate simulations of cochlear excitation and perceptual predictions like loudness and masking. However, at high sound levels relevant to noise exposure, hearing loss, and tinnitus, biomechanical nonlinearities (e.g., in the tympanic membrane or ossicular chain) may become significant. This potential nonlinearity is poorly characterized but could fundamentally impact models of noise-induced damage, hearing aid fitting, and the interpretation of high-level psychoacoustic data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the effective transmission filter of the outer and middle ear exhibits measurable compressive nonlinearity at high sound pressure levels (≥ 90 dB SPL), deviating from the linear transfer function derived from low-level measurements.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-pronged approach. First, we will conduct detailed psychoacoustic experiments (forward masking, loudness matching) across a wide frequency range (0.25–8 kHz) and level range (70–110 dB SPL) in normal-hearing listeners, exploiting individual differences in OME transfer functions. Second, we will train a deep neural network (DNN), specifically a temporal convolutional network (TCN), to learn the mapping between the physical stimulus spectrum and the internal excitation pattern inferred from the psychoacoustic data. By analyzing the DNN's learned filters and their level-dependence, we can infer the effective OME nonlinearity separate from cochlear nonlinearities.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, perceptually grounded quantification of OME nonlinearity at high levels. It will lead to more accurate physiological and perceptual models for high-level sound, improving the diagnosis of noise-induced hearing loss by refining reference standards and enhancing hearing aid algorithms (like deep marginal feedback cancellation) by providing a better model of the forward path for high-intensity inputs.
proposals.novelty: While cochlear nonlinearity is well-studied, systematic investigation of OME nonlinearity using perceptual data is novel. The combination of high-level psychoacoustics, individual differences, and interpretable DNN analysis to disentangle peripheral filter stages represents a significant methodological advance beyond traditional linear systems approaches.

proposals.title: Inverse Loudness Synthesis: A Deep Learning Framework for Generating Sounds to Achieve Target Loudness Patterns in Normal and Impaired Hearing
proposals.motivation: Loudness perception is central to hearing aid fitting and sound design, but current models only predict loudness for a given sound. The inverse problem—designing a sound to produce a specific loudness pattern—remains unsolved. This capability is crucial for developing next-generation hearing aids that can actively sculpt loudness across frequency to compensate for loss and hyperacusis, and for creating auditory stimuli with precise perceptual profiles for tinnitus therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: A deep neural network, trained on the forward mapping between sound spectra and their predicted loudness patterns (using established models like Moore & Glasberg's), can be inverted to synthesize novel sound spectra that accurately and robustly achieve arbitrary target loudness profiles, even for listeners with hearing loss.
proposals.method: We will first train a deep neural network (DNN) as a high-fidelity forward loudness predictor, using spectrograms of diverse sounds (speech, music, noise) as input and their corresponding loudness-time patterns (calculated via a modified Moore-Glasberg model) as output. This trained DNN will then be used as a differentiable component in an inversion loop. An optimization algorithm (e.g., gradient descent or a generative adversarial network framework) will iteratively adjust an input spectrogram to minimize the difference between the DNN's predicted loudness and a predefined target pattern, ultimately synthesizing the required sound.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first generalizable computational method for 'loudness engineering,' directly translating perceptual goals into acoustic signals. It will offer a novel tool for hearing aid algorithm development, enabling personalized loudness normalization. Furthermore, it will create a platform for generating new therapeutic sounds for conditions like tinnitus and hyperacusis, based on desired loudness contours rather than arbitrary spectral shapes.
proposals.novelty: While forward loudness models are mature, inverting them for sound synthesis is a novel, non-trivial optimization challenge not previously addressed. The integration of a DNN-based forward model within a differentiable synthesis framework represents a significant methodological advance over simple lookup or iterative adjustment methods, allowing for complex, time-varying target patterns.

proposals.title: Direct Estimation of Individual Auditory Filter Shapes Using Regularized System Identification of Behavioral Frequency-Selective Masking Data
proposals.motivation: Accurate characterization of the auditory filter is fundamental for models of hearing, speech perception, and hearing loss. Current methods for estimating filter parameters (e.g., the notched-noise method) rely on specific, constrained stimulus sets and assumed filter shapes, which may not capture individual variability or pathologies like noise-induced hearing loss (NIHL) and tinnitus. A more flexible, data-driven approach could reveal subtle filter distortions linked to these conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: Advanced system identification algorithms can directly estimate the shape and bandwidth of individual auditory filters from behavioral masking data without a priori assumptions of a standard roex(p,r) form, and these estimated filters will better correlate with specific audiometric profiles and speech-in-noise difficulties than traditional metrics.
proposals.method: We will collect extensive behavioral data on frequency-selective masking using a wide range of complex masker spectra. The input (masker spectrum) to output (detection threshold) relationship will be modeled using regularized linear system identification techniques, specifically employing Tikhonov regularization or LASSO to estimate a stable, sparse impulse response in the frequency domain. Deep neural network architectures, similar to those used in Moore's prior DNN work for NIHL diagnosis, will be explored as non-linear estimators to capture potential filter asymmetries and level-dependent changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, robust tool for deriving individualized auditory filters, advancing basic psychoacoustic research. It will offer new biomarkers for diagnosing cochlear damage beyond the pure-tone audiogram and could inform the personalized fitting of hearing aids by quantifying specific frequency-resolution deficits.
proposals.novelty: While system identification is standard in engineering, its application to directly infer the auditory filter from arbitrary masking data is novel, moving beyond classic psychophysical reverse-correlation and pre-defined filter models. Integrating modern regularization and machine learning techniques for this specific psychoacoustic problem represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Modeling Auditory Two-Tone Suppression Using Deep Neural Networks to Predict Speech-in-Noise Performance in Hearing Loss
proposals.motivation: Auditory models for hearing loss and hearing aids predominantly focus on the excitation pattern, neglecting the crucial role of suppression (or lateral inhibition) in sharpening spectral contrast and reducing neural crosstalk. This suppression is degraded in cochlear hearing loss, contributing to difficulties in understanding speech in noise, a primary complaint of hearing-impaired listeners. A model that accurately simulates this loss of suppression could better predict real-world listening challenges and inform compensation strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: A deep neural network (DNN) model trained to simulate physiological two-tone suppression in the auditory periphery will more accurately predict the speech-in-noise performance of listeners with and without hearing loss than models based solely on excitation patterns.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage DNN model. The first stage will be a spectro-temporal filterbank simulating cochlear mechanics and auditory nerve responses, incorporating a trainable lateral inhibitory network (LIN) layer to model suppression between adjacent frequency channels. This model will be trained using pre-2026 physiological data on two-tone suppression. The second stage will be a DNN classifier, similar to those used in Moore's prior DNN-based diagnostic work, which takes the model's output as input to predict listener performance on standardized speech-in-noise tests (e.g., HINT, QuickSIN). The model's predictions will be validated against extensive behavioral datasets from listeners with varying degrees of noise-induced hearing loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a computational tool that explicitly links the degradation of a specific physiological mechanism (suppression) to a key perceptual deficit (speech-in-noise understanding). It will offer a more principled, mechanism-based explanation for individual differences in hearing aid outcomes. The model could serve as a novel front-end for hearing aid algorithms, specifically targeting the restoration of lost spectral contrast.
proposals.novelty: While DNNs have been used to diagnose hearing loss and for feedback cancellation, applying them to explicitly model and simulate the physiological process of two-tone suppression is novel. The approach inverts the typical modeling focus from excitation to suppression, offering a new lens through which to analyze auditory processing deficits.

proposals.title: Modeling the Auditory Excitation Patterns of Complex Sounds: Validation with Speech and Music in Normal and Impaired Hearing
proposals.motivation: Excitation pattern models are foundational for understanding auditory perception of simple tones and noises, but their application to complex, dynamic sounds like speech and music remains underdeveloped. Validating and extending these models for real-world stimuli is crucial for improving hearing aid algorithms, diagnosing complex hearing deficits like hidden hearing loss, and understanding auditory scene analysis in noisy environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an extended excitation pattern model, incorporating known non-linearities of the cochlea and temporal integration, can accurately predict the internal representation of complex sounds and that deviations from this model in hearing-impaired listeners will correlate with specific speech-in-noise intelligibility deficits.
proposals.method: We will extend Moore & Glasberg's established excitation pattern calculation method to handle time-varying, multi-component signals. The model will be implemented in MATLAB, incorporating a gammatone filterbank, compressive non-linearity, and adaptation loops. We will validate the model by comparing its outputs to psychoacoustic data from normal-hearing and hearing-impaired participants on tasks involving the detection of target speech or musical notes in complex maskers, leveraging recent deep learning methods (e.g., DNN-based feature analysis) to correlate model outputs with behavioral performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated computational tool for predicting the auditory representation of ecologically valid sounds. It will offer new insights into the peripheral coding deficits underlying real-world listening difficulties, directly informing the next generation of diagnostic tests and hearing aid signal processing strategies, such as more physiologically grounded noise reduction and feedback cancellation.
proposals.novelty: While excitation patterns are well-defined for simple sounds, this project systematically applies and validates the model for full, dynamic speech and music signals, explicitly linking model deviations to functional listening impairments measured with contemporary speech-in-noise tests.

proposals.title: Validity of Auditory Filter Shape Estimation in Listeners with Steeply Sloping High-Frequency Hearing Loss
proposals.motivation: Auditory filter shapes, critical for understanding frequency selectivity and speech intelligibility in noise, are typically estimated using notched-noise masking in listeners with relatively flat hearing loss. However, a significant clinical population, including those with noise-induced hearing loss, presents with steeply sloping audiograms. Pre-2026 methods and models often assume flat thresholds, potentially invalidating filter estimates for these individuals and limiting the personalization of hearing-aid algorithms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that standard auditory filter estimation techniques, which assume a constant detection threshold across the masker bandwidth, produce inaccurate and physiologically implausible filter shapes for listeners with steeply sloping high-frequency loss, due to the violation of the equal-maskability assumption.
proposals.method: We will recruit groups with flat and steeply sloping sensorineural hearing loss. Filter shapes will be estimated at 1 and 2 kHz using a notched-noise masking paradigm with a 2AFC procedure. We will implement and compare two analysis approaches: the standard Patterson (1976) model and a modified model that accounts for the sloping threshold within the masker band, potentially using a piecewise-linear correction. Deep neural network models, similar to those Moore has used for hearing-loss diagnosis, will be trained to predict filter bandwidths from audiometric and psychophysical data to identify non-linear interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the boundary conditions for valid auditory filter measurement, directly impacting models of cochlear impairment and speech perception. It will provide a methodological framework for accurately assessing frequency selectivity in a major clinical subgroup, informing the next generation of hearing-aid signal processing strategies that rely on individualized auditory profiles.
proposals.novelty: While filter shapes are well-studied, systematically testing the core equal-maskability assumption in steeply sloping loss is novel. Combining classic psychophysics with modern deep learning analysis to diagnose the problem represents a new, integrative approach to this long-standing methodological limitation.

proposals.title: Spectral Edge Detection in the Auditory System: Modeling Asymmetric Filtering Using Image Processing Algorithms
proposals.motivation: The auditory system's frequency selectivity, characterized by asymmetric auditory filters (sharper high-frequency slopes), is crucial for sound perception, speech intelligibility in noise, and may be linked to tinnitus generation. Traditional models (e.g., roex) describe this asymmetry but do not explicitly frame it as a computational problem for extracting spectral features. This limits our ability to model how the auditory system parses complex sounds, a core interest in Brian C.J. Moore's work on frequency analysis and hearing loss.
proposals.hypothesis: The asymmetry of the auditory filterbank can be functionally modeled as a spectral edge-detection operator, analogous to spatial edge detection in image processing, where the steeper high-frequency slope enhances the representation of rising spectral edges (formant onsets, harmonic boundaries).
proposals.method: We will implement a computational model where a cochleagram (simulated using a gammatone filterbank) is treated as a spectro-temporal 'image'. Pre-2026 edge detection algorithms (Sobel, Canny) will be adapted to operate along the spectral (frequency) axis. The model's asymmetric filtering will be parameterized to mimic physiological data. We will test its performance against psychoacoustic data on frequency discrimination and profile analysis, and evaluate its utility in enhancing speech features in noise for listeners with simulated hearing loss, using deep neural network-based speech intelligibility predictors (e.g., DNSMOS).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework linking auditory physiology to a well-established signal processing paradigm, offering new insights into spectral contrast coding. It could lead to improved algorithms for hearing aid signal processing that enhance spectral edges to compensate for broadened auditory filters in hearing loss, directly extending Moore's recent work on deep learning for hearing diagnostics and feedback cancellation.
proposals.novelty: While auditory filters are well-studied, conceptualizing and implementing their collective action as a spectral edge-detection system is a novel cross-domain analogy. Applying concrete image processing operators to model this function, and testing it with both psychoacoustic and speech-in-noise metrics, represents a significant departure from standard auditory modeling approaches.

proposals.title: Level Dependence of the Broadband 'Tail' Filter in Auditory Frequency Selectivity: A Test of Gain and Shape Invariance
proposals.motivation: Auditory filter shapes, critical for understanding frequency selectivity and hearing loss, are typically derived from notched-noise masking experiments. The 'tail' of the filter, representing low-frequency response, is often assumed to be invariant with sound level, but this is based on limited data. Confirming or refuting this invariance is essential for accurate models of loudness perception, speech intelligibility in noise, and the underlying cochlear mechanics, particularly for listeners with hearing loss where level-dependent changes may be exaggerated.
proposals.hypothesis: The gain and/or shape of the broader, low-frequency tail of the auditory filter is not invariant with sound level, exhibiting systematic changes that differ between normal-hearing listeners and those with sensorineural hearing loss.
proposals.method: We will employ a forward-masking paradigm with a notched-noise masker to estimate auditory filters at multiple probe levels (e.g., 30, 50, 70 dB SPL) for center frequencies of 1 and 4 kHz. For normal-hearing and hearing-impaired participants, filter shapes will be derived by fitting a roex(p,w,t) model to the masking data using a least-squares minimization algorithm. The key analysis will involve statistically comparing the derived tail parameters (gain 't' and slope 'w') across levels and groups, controlling for individual differences in absolute threshold.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic test of level invariance for the tail filter, directly informing computational models of auditory processing (e.g., the Cambridge loudness model). It will clarify whether current clinical and psychoacoustic assumptions about frequency selectivity hold across stimulus levels, with implications for hearing aid signal processing strategies that rely on accurate filterbank representations.
proposals.novelty: While level dependence of the filter tip is established, the tail's properties have been treated as a fixed, low-gain feature. This study explicitly challenges that simplification, integrating Moore's expertise in individual differences and auditory filter modeling to test a foundational but underexamined assumption.

proposals.title: A Model of Cochlear Gain Control Based on Integrated Cochlear Excitation: Implications for Hearing Loss and Tinnitus
proposals.motivation: Current models of the cochlear 'tip filter' or active gain control in hearing aids and auditory models typically adjust gain based on the instantaneous level of the signal in a specific frequency channel. This fails to capture the physiological reality of the medial olivocochlear (MOC) efferent system, which provides gain control based on integrated neural activity across a broad frequency range. A more accurate model is needed to understand phenomena like loudness perception in complex sounds, reduced frequency selectivity in hearing loss, and the generation of tinnitus, where global cochlear excitation patterns are altered.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling cochlear filter gain as a function of total cochlear excitation, rather than local signal level, will more accurately simulate auditory perception in normal and impaired listeners, particularly for complex sounds like speech in noise and for conditions associated with hyperacusis and tinnitus.
proposals.method: We will develop and test a computational auditory model that extends established dual-resonance nonlinear (DRNL) filterbank architectures. The gain parameter for each frequency-specific DRNL filter will be dynamically controlled by a weighted sum of the outputs from all filters, simulating integrated cochlear excitation via MOC feedback. This model will be implemented in MATLAB and validated using pre-2026 psychoacoustic data on frequency selectivity, intensity discrimination, and speech-in-noise perception from both normal-hearing listeners and individuals with sensorineural hearing loss and tinnitus.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more physiologically plausible computational framework for cochlear processing, directly linking efferent feedback mechanisms to perceptual outcomes. It will offer new insights into the perceptual consequences of hearing loss and generate testable predictions for the role of global excitation in tinnitus generation and hyperacusis, potentially informing future hearing-aid gain-control strategies.
proposals.novelty: While MOC effects are well-documented, implementing a real-time, filterbank-wide integrated excitation signal to control individual channel gain within a mainstream auditory model is a novel computational approach that moves beyond local automatic gain control (AGC) approximations.

proposals.title: Validation of the Tip/Tail Auditory Filter Model Using Complex, Ecologically Relevant Stimuli
proposals.motivation: The tip/tail filter model, a key concept in auditory psychophysics, has been primarily validated using simple tonal stimuli. However, real-world auditory processing involves complex sounds like speech and noise. Testing the model's predictions with such stimuli is crucial for confirming its biological plausibility and relevance to hearing aid and cochlear implant signal processing, which must operate effectively in complex acoustic environments.
proposals.hypothesis: The perceptual effects predicted by the asymmetric tip/tail auditory filter model for complex stimuli (narrowband noise, speech) will align with empirical psychoacoustic data, whereas predictions from symmetric filter models will show significant deviations.
proposals.method: We will conduct a series of psychoacoustic experiments with normal-hearing and hearing-impaired participants. Using adaptive procedures, we will measure detection and discrimination thresholds for target signals (e.g., a tone or speech phoneme) embedded in spectrally shaped maskers. The data will be compared against predictions generated by computational implementations of the tip/tail filter (using a roex(p,r) or gammachirp formulation) and standard symmetric filters. Stimuli will include harmonic complexes, narrowband noises, and consonant-vowel syllables, with analysis focusing on frequency and temporal masking patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a critical test of a foundational auditory model using ecologically valid sounds, strengthening the link between basic psychoacoustics and applied hearing science. The findings will directly inform the design of more physiologically realistic front-end filters for deep neural network-based hearing aid algorithms, such as those for feedback cancellation and noise reduction, improving their performance with speech and music.
proposals.novelty: While the tip/tail model is established, systematic validation against complex, non-tonal stimuli is lacking. This study uniquely bridges high-fidelity psychophysical modeling with the complex signal processing challenges faced in real-world assistive listening devices.

proposals.title: A Level-Dependent Attenuation Model of Auditory Filtering: Implications for Speech Intelligibility in Noise and Hearing Aid Processing
proposals.motivation: Traditional models of auditory filtering, such as the gammatone or roex filters, conceptualize the filter as amplifying the target signal within its passband. This framework, while successful for normal hearing, may not accurately represent the altered auditory processing in hearing loss, where cochlear gain is reduced. A paradigm shift, viewing the filter as attenuating the masker outside its passband, could provide a more physiologically grounded model of impaired hearing and lead to more effective noise-reduction strategies for hearing aids.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling the auditory filter as a level-dependent attenuator of masker energy, rather than a signal amplifier, will better predict speech-in-noise intelligibility for listeners with sensorineural hearing loss, particularly for spectro-temporally complex maskers.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational auditory model where the standard gammatone filterbank is replaced by a dual-process filter. This filter will apply level-dependent attenuation to masker components, with attenuation slopes derived from psychoacoustic notched-noise masking data (e.g., from Moore & Glasberg, 1987). The model's output will be a 'masker-attenuated' excitation pattern. We will test predictions against existing datasets of speech reception thresholds in steady and fluctuating noise for normal-hearing and hearing-impaired listeners. The model will be integrated into a deep neural network-based speech intelligibility predictor, similar to those used in Moore's recent work on hearing aid feedback cancellation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel theoretical framework for understanding auditory scene analysis in hearing impairment. It will directly contribute to the design of next-generation hearing aid algorithms by offering a biologically inspired blueprint for noise reduction that prioritizes the attenuation of competing sounds over the problematic amplification of a degraded target signal.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the conceptual inversion of the filter's role from signal-centric to masker-centric, explicitly linking filter shape to level-dependent compression loss. This contrasts with all standard auditory filter models and aligns with emerging pre-2026 views of hearing loss as a deficit in suppressing background noise.

proposals.title: Predicting Speech Intelligibility for Hearing-Impaired Listeners: A Model-Based Framework for Selecting Acoustic Modifications in Hearing Aids
proposals.motivation: Hearing aid algorithms apply various acoustic modifications (e.g., frequency compression, noise reduction) to improve listening, but their impact on speech intelligibility is difficult to predict and varies across individuals. Current selection methods rely on trial-and-error fitting or generic prescriptions, which are inefficient and may not optimize outcomes. A model-driven approach, grounded in auditory psychophysics, could provide a principled method for personalizing hearing aid processing.
proposals.hypothesis: The intelligibility impact of specific, time-varying acoustic modifications can be accurately predicted for individual listeners with hearing loss by integrating a computational model of their impaired auditory processing (e.g., the Cambridge loudness and auditory filter models) with a speech intelligibility index (SII) framework.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage model. First, an 'auditory front-end' will simulate individual peripheral hearing loss using pre-2026 models (e.g., Moore & Glasberg's dynamic loudness model and roex auditory filters fitted to individual audiograms and temporal modulation data). Second, a modified version of the Extended Speech Intelligibility Index (ESII) will be applied to the simulated auditory representation of modified speech signals. The model will be validated by comparing its predictions against behavioral intelligibility scores from listeners with varying hearing loss profiles for speech processed with a controlled set of modifications (e.g., multichannel compression, spectral shaping).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, theoretically grounded tool for hearing aid fitting, moving beyond black-box optimization. It will advance the field by explicitly linking specific auditory deficits to the efficacy of signal processing strategies. The resulting framework could be integrated into future fitting software to guide clinicians in selecting modifications that are predicted to maximize intelligibility for a given patient.
proposals.novelty: While models exist for normal hearing and some for impaired loudness, the integration of a personalized, dynamic auditory model with an intelligibility index to predict the effect of *active hearing aid processing* on a per-individual basis is a significant and untested step. This moves beyond using models merely for diagnosis or simulating loss, to actively guiding remediation.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Efficacy of Integrated Audiovisual Enhancement Strategies for Speech Intelligibility in Listeners with Sensorineural Hearing Loss
proposals.motivation: For individuals with hearing loss, especially noise-induced loss, communication remains challenging in complex acoustic environments despite advanced hearing aids. Pre-2026 research shows that auditory cues alone are often insufficient, and listeners naturally supplement with visual speechreading. A systematic, device-integrated approach combining optimized acoustic modifications with complementary visual cues could provide a more robust perceptual solution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a co-adapted strategy, where real-time acoustic speech modifications (e.g., frequency lowering) are paired with synchronized, simplified visual speech cues (e.g., enhanced lip movements), will yield significantly greater improvements in speech-in-noise intelligibility for hearing-impaired listeners than either modality in isolation.
proposals.method: We will develop a closed-loop testing platform using a deep neural network (DNN) architecture for real-time audio processing, implementing frequency-selective amplification and non-linear frequency compression tailored to individual audiograms. This will be integrated with a visual signal processing module that extracts and stylizes key articulatory features from a speaker's video feed. Using a repeated-measures design, we will assess speech reception thresholds in noise across three conditions: audio-only (with DNN processing), visual-only (enhanced), and the integrated audiovisual condition. Participants will be adults with mild-to-moderate sensorineural hearing loss, including veterans with suspected noise-induced loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for designing multi-modal hearing assistance strategies, moving beyond purely auditory solutions. It will yield novel data on the optimal coupling of specific acoustic and visual cue enhancements, directly informing the next generation of communication devices and rehabilitation protocols.
proposals.novelty: While multi-sensory integration is known, the novelty lies in the real-time, co-adaptive algorithmic pairing of specific hearing-loss-compensatory audio processing with engineered visual supplements, creating a unified enhancement strategy rather than presenting two independent streams of information.

proposals.title: Personalized Speech Enhancement via Deep Neural Networks Using Individualized Auditory Profiles and Listening Histories
proposals.motivation: Current hearing aids and speech enhancement systems apply broad compensation strategies based on the audiogram, neglecting crucial individual differences in supra-threshold processing, frequency selectivity, and temporal fine structure coding. This one-size-fits-all approach fails to optimize speech intelligibility and quality for listeners with diverse etiologies (e.g., noise-induced loss vs. age-related loss) and listening histories, particularly in complex acoustic environments. A more granular, personalized model is needed to move beyond the audiogram.
proposals.hypothesis: A deep neural network (DNN) system trained on a rich set of individualized auditory metrics—including not just audiometric thresholds but also measures of frequency selectivity, temporal modulation detection, and estimated lifetime noise exposure—will generate personalized speech modifications that yield superior improvements in speech reception thresholds and subjective sound quality compared to systems based solely on the audiogram.
proposals.method: We will develop a DNN architecture that takes as input a multi-dimensional listener profile (audiogram, notched-noise-derived frequency selectivity, temporal modulation transfer function, and a noise-exposure history questionnaire score) and a noisy speech signal. The network, building on pre-2025 deep marginal feedback cancellation and speech enhancement architectures, will be trained end-to-end to output a cleaned and spectro-temporally tailored speech signal. Training and validation will use a database of speech-in-noise signals, with target outputs optimized for intelligibility (via a pre-existing DNN-based intelligibility metric) and quality. The system's efficacy will be evaluated against a state-of-the-art audiogram-based hearing aid algorithm using participants with varied hearing loss profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete framework and proof-of-concept for 'auditory profile-driven' audio processing, shifting the field from threshold-based correction towards holistic auditory remediation. It will deliver a validated DNN model that can be integrated into next-generation hearing aid platforms. The findings will also elucidate which non-audiometric factors are most critical for personalizing speech enhancement.
proposals.novelty: While DNNs are used for speech enhancement, their conditioning on a comprehensive, individualized auditory profile—integrating both physiological (psychoacoustic) and historical (noise exposure) data—is a novel approach. This moves beyond using the audiogram as a simple gain table to using a rich profile to steer the fundamental transformation of the speech signal itself.

proposals.title: Superior Speech-in-Noise Perception in High-Functioning Autism: Characterizing Auditory Profiles and Underlying Mechanisms
proposals.motivation: Research on auditory processing in Autism Spectrum Conditions (ASC) has often focused on deficits, particularly in complex listening tasks like speech-in-noise (SiN). However, anecdotal and emerging pre-2026 evidence suggests a subset of individuals may exhibit superior abilities. This proposal directly inverts the deficit assumption to systematically identify and characterize this subgroup, which is crucial for moving beyond a uniform model of auditory processing in ASC and for developing personalized auditory support strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: A distinct subgroup of high-functioning autistic (HFA/AS) individuals will demonstrate superior speech-in-noise perception compared to neurotypical controls, and this superiority will be associated with enhanced temporal fine-structure coding and/or more efficient auditory grouping mechanisms, rather than being a simple consequence of hypersensitivity.
proposals.method: We will recruit HFA/AS and matched control participants. Core assessments include: 1) Standard and high-frequency audiometry to rule out peripheral loss. 2) SiN tests (e.g., QuickSIN, BKB-SIN) across noise types (steady-state, modulated, competing talkers). 3) Psychoacoustic measures of temporal fine structure (TFS) sensitivity (e.g., binaural TFS tasks, monaural frequency discrimination at 2 kHz) and auditory grouping (e.g., comodulation masking release). 4) A deep neural network (DNN) model, pre-trained on speech and adapted using a SiN loss function, will analyze individual auditory profiles (integrating audiometric, temporal, and grouping data) to classify performance subgroups and identify key predictive features.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, mechanism-driven evidence for a 'superior listener' phenotype within the autism spectrum, challenging the prevailing deficit narrative. It will delineate the specific auditory processing advantages (e.g., in TFS or grouping) that underpin this ability, offering a new framework for understanding heterogeneous auditory profiles in ASC and informing targeted interventions.
proposals.novelty: The proposal explicitly inverts the dominant deficit model in autism auditory research to test for a superior ability. It combines established psychoacoustic probes of TFS and grouping—core themes in Moore's pre-2026 work—with a modern DNN-based profiling approach to move beyond group averages and identify meaningful individual differences.

proposals.title: The Impact of Real-World Reverberation on Speech-in-Noise Perception: Individual Differences in Listeners with and without Tinnitus
proposals.motivation: Standard speech-in-noise (SIN) tests are conducted in non-reverberant chambers, which poorly represent everyday listening environments. Reverberation, a key feature of real-world spaces like offices and restaurants, degrades temporal and spectral speech cues, potentially exacerbating difficulties for hearing-impaired listeners and those with tinnitus. Understanding its specific impact is crucial for developing more ecologically valid diagnostic tools and hearing-aid algorithms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that real-world reverberation will disproportionately impair SIN performance compared to anechoic conditions, and that this impairment will be greater for individuals with hearing loss and/or tinnitus, correlating with specific deficits in temporal fine structure processing.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled study comparing SIN thresholds (using the Matrix or QuickSIN test) in an anechoic chamber versus a configurable reverberant room (T60 ~0.6s). Participants will include groups with normal hearing, sensorineural hearing loss, and tinnitus (with and without hearing loss). Performance will be analyzed against individual psychoacoustic measures of temporal resolution (e.g., gap detection) and frequency selectivity. Deep neural network-based feature extraction (using pre-2026 architectures like CNNs) will be applied to the acoustic stimuli to quantify the degradation of temporal and spectral modulations caused by reverberation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for linking real-world acoustic degradation to individual auditory profiles, moving beyond standard clinical SIN tests. It will inform the next generation of hearing-aid processing, particularly deep learning-based noise reduction and feedback cancellation systems, by specifying the types of signal distortion they must compensate for in reverberant spaces.
proposals.novelty: While reverberant SIN testing exists, this proposal uniquely integrates it with detailed individual difference analysis (temporal processing, tinnitus status) and uses contemporary DNN-based acoustic analysis to objectively characterize the challenging signal properties, bridging a gap between basic psychoacoustics and applied hearing-aid research.

proposals.title: Pupillometry and Gaze as Objective Indicators of Listening Effort and Speech Segregation in Hearing-Impaired Listeners
proposals.motivation: Understanding speech in noise is a primary challenge for individuals with hearing loss, but traditional measures like word recognition scores fail to capture the cognitive effort involved. Pre-2026 research has established pupillometry as a sensitive, objective measure of listening effort, and eye-tracking can reveal strategies for segregating target speech from competing talkers. Combining these measures offers a more complete picture of the perceptual and cognitive load experienced during real-world listening.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that during a speech-in-noise task with spatially separated talkers, individuals with hearing loss will exhibit larger task-evoked pupil dilation (indicating greater effort) and more systematic gaze patterns towards the target talker's location compared to normal-hearing listeners, and that these oculomotor metrics will correlate with individual differences in frequency resolution and temporal processing.
proposals.method: Participants with and without sensorineural hearing loss will perform a coordinate response measure (CRM) speech-in-noise task with target and masker talkers at different azimuths. Eye movements and pupil diameter will be recorded using a head-mounted eye-tracker (e.g., Pupil Labs). The speech stimuli will be processed using a hearing loss simulation based on individual audiograms. We will analyze task-evoked pupil responses (TEPRs) and compute gaze entropy and target-directed gaze probability. Deep neural network models (e.g., LSTMs) will be trained to predict subjective effort ratings from the combined pupillometry and gaze time-series data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, multi-faceted objective assessment of listening effort and auditory attention in hearing impairment. It will establish links between specific oculomotor behaviors, auditory processing deficits, and perceived difficulty, offering new tools for evaluating hearing aid algorithms and rehabilitation strategies. The findings will directly inform the development of more ecologically valid tests of hearing aid benefit.
proposals.novelty: While pupillometry has been used alone, the simultaneous analysis of gaze patterns for spatial attention and pupil dilation for cognitive load, specifically in hearing-impaired listeners performing a realistic speech task, is novel. Integrating these measures with individual psychoacoustic profiles and applying modern sequence modeling (LSTMs) to predict effort represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Parametric Control of Temporal and Spectral Features in Synthetic Noise to Isolate Perceptual Cues for Tinnitus and Speech-in-Noise Difficulties
proposals.motivation: Individuals with hearing loss and tinnitus often struggle with complex sounds like speech in noise, but the specific acoustic features driving these difficulties remain poorly understood. Using complex, naturalistic noises makes it hard to isolate the contributions of specific temporal and spectral modulations. A parametric approach using simplified sounds can directly test theories of cochlear damage and neural plasticity underlying these common auditory disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: That deficits in processing specific, parametrically defined temporal envelopes and spectral profiles will correlate strongly with self-reported tinnitus distress and speech-in-noise performance, independent of pure-tone audiometric thresholds.
proposals.method: We will generate synthetic noise stimuli with independent parametric control over temporal modulation rate/depth and spectral profile (e.g., notch width/center, slope). Using adaptive psychophysical procedures, we will measure detection and discrimination thresholds for these features in groups with normal hearing, sensorineural hearing loss, and tinnitus. Deep neural network models (e.g., a convolutional-recurrent architecture) pre-trained on speech and environmental sounds will be used to analyze the feature salience of our stimuli, linking perceptual data to computational representations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct link between perceptual deficits for specific, quantifiable acoustic features and real-world auditory complaints. It will provide a novel, fine-grained diagnostic tool that goes beyond the audiogram, identifying specific processing deficits. The findings will inform the development of more targeted auditory training and sound therapy protocols by identifying the key stimulus parameters needing remediation.
proposals.novelty: While noise stimuli are used in hearing research, the systematic, independent parametric control of temporal and spectral features for direct perceptual testing is novel. Combining this with DNN analysis to interpret perceptual results within a model of complex sound processing bridges a gap between basic psychophysics and modern computational auditory neuroscience.

proposals.title: The Auditory Figure-Ground Problem: Quantifying the Perceptual Salience of Background Noise During Attended Speech
proposals.motivation: Understanding speech in noise is a primary challenge for hearing-impaired listeners and hearing-aid users. However, the perception of the background noise itself, when attention is focused on speech, is poorly understood. This 'dual perception' is crucial for assessing the naturalness and listening fatigue associated with noise-reduction algorithms in hearing aids, which can introduce processing artifacts into the residual background.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the perceptual salience of a background sound is systematically degraded when a listener successfully attends to a foreground target talker, and that this degradation is more pronounced in listeners with cochlear hearing loss due to reduced frequency selectivity and increased susceptibility to masking.
proposals.method: We will adapt a dual-task psychophysical paradigm. Listeners with normal and impaired hearing will attend to a target sentence in modulated noise or a competing talker background. In catch trials, the background will be replaced by a probe sound (e.g., a change in modulation rate, frequency, or a brief gap). Detection thresholds for these background changes will be measured. We will employ a deep neural network (DNN) model, pre-trained on auditory scenes (inspired by Moore's recent DNN work on hearing loss diagnosis), to extract multi-scale spectro-temporal features.
