A logistic regression classifier will use these features to predict the psychophysical detection data, identifying the acoustic cues governing background salience.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative model of background sound perception under divided attention, linking acoustic features to perceptual salience. It will establish a novel behavioral metric for evaluating the ecological validity of hearing-aid signal processing, moving beyond simple speech intelligibility to assess the perceptual integrity of the entire auditory scene.
proposals.novelty: While foreground speech perception is well-studied, the concurrent perception of the background is not. This proposal inverts the classic 'speech-in-noise' problem to study 'noise-in-speech' perception, combining a novel dual-task behavioral design with a DNN-based feature analysis grounded in auditory physiology.

proposals.title: The Role of Temporal Fine Structure in Speech-in-Quiet Perception: A Test Using Frequency-Shifted Vocoders
proposals.motivation: The importance of temporal fine structure (TFS) cues for speech perception in noise and for pitch perception is well-established in pre-2026 literature. However, its contribution to speech intelligibility in quiet, where envelope cues are fully available, remains unclear and debated. Resolving this is critical for refining models of auditory processing and for informing the design of hearing aids and cochlear implants, which often degrade or discard TFS information.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that TFS information provides no significant benefit to the intelligibility of speech presented in quiet for listeners with normal hearing, once sufficient spectral (place) and envelope information is preserved.
proposals.method: We will test normal-hearing participants using vocoded speech, a well-established pre-2026 method. Speech will be processed through a 32-channel noise vocoder. Crucially, we will systematically degrade TFS by shifting the carrier noise bands relative to their original center frequencies (e.g., by a factor of 1.2 or 1.5), a technique validated in Moore's prior work on frequency discrimination. Performance on monosyllabic word and sentence tests in quiet will be compared between conditions with preserved TFS (original band frequencies) and degraded TFS (frequency-shifted bands), while carefully controlling for envelope and spectral profile cues.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, quantitative assessment of the necessity of TFS for speech perception in an ideal listening scenario. It will directly inform theoretical debates on the functional roles of different temporal cues and offer practical guidance on the minimal signal processing requirements for auditory prostheses in quiet environments.
proposals.novelty: While TFS has been studied extensively in noise, this proposal directly targets the unresolved question of its role in quiet, a fundamental listening condition. The use of frequency-shifted vocoders, a method refined in Moore's lab for probing phase-locking limits, provides a novel and precise tool to isolate the TFS contribution from other cues in this context.

proposals.title: The Role of Temporal Fine Structure (TFS) Cues in Speech-in-Noise and Speech-in-Reverberation Perception: A Combined Psychoacoustic and Deep Learning Analysis
proposals.motivation: Understanding speech in complex, realistic environments like reverberant rooms or with competing talkers remains a major challenge for individuals with hearing loss, even when audibility is restored. Pre-2026 research by Moore and others suggests that deficits in processing Temporal Fine Structure (TFS) information may be a key factor, but its specific contribution to these complex listening tasks is not fully quantified. This limits the development of hearing aid algorithms that could selectively enhance or preserve TFS cues to improve real-world listening.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an individual's ability to utilize TFS cues, as measured by a baseline psychoacoustic task, will be a significant predictor of their performance in understanding speech in the presence of reverberation and a competing talker, and that this relationship will be stronger than predictions based on pure-tone audiometry alone.
proposals.method: We will recruit a group of listeners with normal hearing and mild-to-moderate sensorineural hearing loss. All participants will complete a TFS sensitivity test (e.g., a monaural frequency discrimination task at 2 kHz or a TFS-lowF task). Speech reception thresholds (SRTs) will be measured for sentences in two complex conditions: a competing talker and a reverberant environment. We will then train a deep neural network (DNN) regression model, using architectures similar to those in Moore's prior DNN work for hearing loss diagnosis, to predict the SRTs. Input features will include pure-tone thresholds, age, and the TFS sensitivity measure. The model's performance and feature importance analysis will quantify the unique contribution of TFS sensitivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, quantitative link between a psychoacoustic measure of TFS processing and performance in ecologically valid, complex listening scenarios. It will advance the theoretical understanding of auditory object formation in challenging environments. The findings will directly inform the design of next-generation hearing aids and cochlear implants by identifying which listeners are most likely to benefit from signal processing strategies that preserve or enhance TFS information.
proposals.novelty: While TFS importance is established for simple tasks, this study uniquely combines a robust TFS sensitivity metric with complex speech-in-noise/reverberation tests and uses a data-driven DNN approach to model their relationship, moving beyond simple correlation to identify predictive features for real-world listening difficulty.

proposals.title: Real-Time Enhancement of Temporal Fine Structure in Hearing Aids: A Low-Latency Implementation and Evaluation with Speech in Noise
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including work by Moore and others, highlights the critical role of Temporal Fine Structure (TFS) cues for understanding speech in complex, fluctuating noise and for sound localization. However, hearing aid processing often degrades these cues due to channel summation, compression, and inherent delays. Implementing real-time TFS enhancement in a wearable device remains a significant, unsolved engineering challenge with direct implications for user satisfaction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a low-complexity, low-latency algorithm for TFS extraction and selective enhancement can be implemented on a real-time hearing aid platform, leading to measurable improvements in speech-in-noise intelligibility and subjective sound quality for listeners with hearing loss, compared to standard wide-dynamic-range compression (WDRC) processing.
proposals.method: We will develop a real-time system on a research hearing aid platform (e.g., the open-source OpenMHA). The core algorithm will adapt a low-latency TFS extraction method, such as a modified Hilbert transform or zero-crossing analysis within auditory filterbanks. This TFS information will guide a gain rule that selectively applies minimal gain in channels with high TFS-to-envelope coherence, preserving natural fluctuations, while applying stronger WDRC in channels dominated by noise. We will evaluate performance using objective metrics (e.g., HASQI), and behavioral tests with hearing-impaired participants using sentence-in-noise materials (e.g., IEEE or CRM sentences) in a laboratory setting.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first proof-of-concept for a real-time, biologically-inspired hearing aid algorithm that explicitly enhances TFS cues. It will deliver a benchmark for the achievable latency and processing load of such algorithms on current hardware and establish a direct link between real-time TFS manipulation and functional listening benefits.
proposals.novelty: While the importance of TFS is known, its real-time exploitation in hearing aids is novel. The novelty lies in the specific low-latency implementation strategy and the integrated evaluation on a wearable platform, moving beyond offline simulations to test practical feasibility and immediate perceptual effects.

proposals.title: Simulating Sensorineural Hearing Impairment in Normal-Hearing Listeners: Selective Degradation of Temporal Fine Structure and its Impact on Speech Perception in Noise
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Moore's work, indicates that sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL) degrades the neural encoding of temporal fine structure (TFS), a crucial cue for understanding speech in fluctuating noise and perceiving pitch. However, the specific contribution of TFS degradation to real-world listening difficulties remains partially confounded by co-occurring loss of frequency selectivity and audibility in clinical populations. A controlled simulation of this deficit in normal-hearing listeners is needed to isolate its perceptual consequences.
proposals.hypothesis: Artificially degrading TFS information in normal-hearing listeners, using a validated signal-processing algorithm, will produce speech-in-noise perception deficits and psychoacoustic performance patterns that mimic those observed in listeners with mild-to-moderate SNHL, even when audibility is fully preserved.
proposals.method: We will implement a real-time auditory processing model to selectively degrade TFS while preserving envelope cues. The core algorithm will be a 'TFS-smearing' process based on the approach of Lorenzi et al. (2006), implemented within a deep neural network (DNN) framework for precise, adaptive control. Normal-hearing participants will perform speech-in-noise tests (e.g., CRM or HINT) and psychoacoustic tasks (e.g., frequency discrimination at 2 kHz, binaural masking level differences) under three conditions: unprocessed audio, noise-vocoded audio (envelope-only control), and TFS-degraded audio. Performance will be compared to pre-2026 data from hearing-impaired listeners.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a causal, parametric model of how TFS loss contributes to the hearing-impaired perceptual experience, decoupled from other cochlear pathologies. It will validate a novel tool for simulating hearing impairment, which can be used to train audiologists, design better hearing-aid algorithms (informed by DNN-based feedback cancellation research), and develop more targeted auditory training protocols.
proposals.novelty: While noise-vocoding simulates cochlear implant listening, a method to selectively degrade TFS to simulate SNHL in normal-hearing ears is less established. Combining the precise TFS manipulation algorithm with a DNN-based implementation and a comprehensive battery of temporal and speech tests, directly linking to Moore's prior work on frequency discrimination and hearing loss diagnosis, represents a novel synthesis.

proposals.title: Testing the Constancy of Perceptual Internal Noise: A Cross-Modal, Model-Based Approach Using Auditory and Visual Tasks
proposals.motivation: The concept of 'internal noise' is fundamental to models of perceptual performance, from hearing to vision, often used to explain individual differences in tasks like frequency discrimination or speech-in-noise perception. However, its measurement is typically circular, inferred from the very task it is used to explain. This project directly addresses this foundational problem by testing whether internal noise is a stable, global property of an individual or a variable characteristic of specific sensory systems, with implications for models of hearing loss and tinnitus.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that internal noise is not a unitary construct; instead, it is modality-specific. Therefore, a computational model with separate internal noise parameters for auditory and visual tasks will provide a significantly better fit to an individual's cross-modal performance data than a model constrained to use a single, global noise parameter.
proposals.method: We will measure perceptual thresholds in two modalities: (1) Auditory: pure-tone frequency discrimination at 2 kHz (leveraging Moore's expertise) and speech-in-noise reception thresholds. (2) Visual: orientation discrimination of Gabor patches. For each participant, data will be fit using an ideal observer framework (e.g., a linear amplifier model) implemented in a hierarchical Bayesian model. The key comparison will be between a model where internal noise (σ_int) is a single, free parameter shared across all tasks, and a model where σ_int is allowed to vary independently for auditory (frequency & speech) and visual tasks. Model comparison will use Bayes factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, model-based test of the constancy of internal noise across sensory modalities. It will clarify a core theoretical concept in psychophysics and offer a new methodological framework for dissociating sensory-specific from generalized cognitive contributions to perceptual performance, relevant for understanding individual variability in hearing-impaired listeners.
proposals.novelty: While internal noise is widely modeled, its cross-modal constancy has never been rigorously tested. The novelty lies in combining auditory and visual psychophysics within a single, formal model-comparison framework to break the circularity of inferring noise from a single task.

proposals.title: Dynamic Internal Noise in Auditory Timing: A Cross-Correlation Model Analysis of Behavioral and Neural Data
proposals.motivation: Internal noise is a fundamental limit in sensory and cognitive processing, often modeled as constant in auditory psychophysics. However, its dynamic nature in timing tasks, which rely on neural cross-correlation or autocorrelation mechanisms for interval encoding, remains poorly understood. Quantifying how this noise varies with task demands and neural resource allocation is crucial for accurate models of hearing, speech perception, and conditions like tinnitus where temporal processing is disrupted.
proposals.hypothesis: Internal noise within the auditory timing system is not constant but scales dynamically with task sensitivity (e.g., discrimination vs. production) and temporal context, as revealed by parameter fluctuations in fitted neural cross-correlation models.
proposals.method: We will collect high-precision behavioral data (reaction times, accuracy) and concurrent EEG (focusing on evoked potentials like the N1/P2 and auditory steady-state responses) from participants performing interval discrimination and production tasks across a range of frequencies. The data will be fit with established computational models of neural cross-correlation (e.g., the Mismatch Negativity (MMN) paradigm for deviance detection, and brainstem model fitting techniques). Model parameters, including noise variance, will be estimated trial-by-trial using Bayesian hierarchical modeling (e.g., Stan) to assess dynamic fluctuations linked to task performance and neural signatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, model-based evidence for dynamic internal noise in auditory timing, moving beyond static assumptions. It will establish a novel framework linking computational principles of timing (cross-correlation) to measurable neural and behavioral variability, with implications for understanding individual differences in hearing loss and temporal processing deficits in tinnitus.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely uses the theoretical timing mechanism (neural cross-correlation) as the tool to measure its own noise dynamics, rather than treating noise as an external additive parameter. This directly unifies the study of neural computation with psychophysical noise models in a way not previously attempted.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating the Constancy of Internal Noise in Auditory Perception: A Multi-Measure and Multi-Task Approach
proposals.motivation: A foundational assumption in models of auditory perception, including those for frequency discrimination and speech-in-noise understanding, is that internal noise is a fixed, intrinsic property of the auditory system. This assumption constrains model predictions and interpretations of individual differences, particularly in hearing-impaired and tinnitus populations. If internal noise is not constant but varies with stimulus context or cognitive load, current diagnostic and rehabilitative strategies, such as hearing aid fitting, may be suboptimal.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that internal noise is not a fixed property but varies systematically with the sensitivity of the measurement paradigm, the spectral-temporal complexity of the stimulus, and the listener's attentional state. Specifically, more sensitive measures and ecologically valid tasks will reveal greater variability and higher levels of internal noise than standard psychoacoustic tasks.
proposals.method: We will employ a within-subjects design with normal-hearing, hearing-impaired, and tinnitus groups. Internal noise will be estimated using: 1) High-precision adaptive frequency discrimination tasks (2 kHz) with trial-by-trial feedback analysis to track learning and variability. 2) A 'double-pass' consistency method applied to speech-in-noise recognition (using matrix sentence tests) to separate sensory noise from decision noise. 3) A novel temporal integration task requiring detection of gaps in modulated noise, analyzed using a computational model (e.g., a modified version of the MATLAB-based Auditory Image Model) to infer internal noise in the temporal domain. Deep neural network analysis of individual response patterns (similar to pre-2026 DNN methods for hearing loss diagnosis) will be used to identify latent predictors of internal noise variability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated, multi-dimensional assessment of internal noise, challenging a key simplifying assumption in auditory modeling. It will offer a new framework for interpreting individual differences in hearing ability, potentially linking variability in internal noise to specific perceptual deficits in hearing loss and tinnitus. The findings could inform the development of next-generation hearing aids that adapt not just to gain but to estimated internal noise levels.
proposals.novelty: While internal noise is often treated as a fitted constant, directly testing its constancy across disparate, high-sensitivity perceptual domains (spectral, speech-based, temporal) within the same individuals is novel. Combining classic psychophysics with consistency methods and computational modeling to attack this core assumption represents a significant methodological advance over single-paradigm studies.

proposals.title: Perceptual Learning for the Detection of a Complex Masker in a Tonal Background: Implications for Tinnitus and Auditory Training
proposals.motivation: Traditional auditory masking paradigms treat a simple signal (e.g., a tone) as the target within a complex masker, modeling everyday listening challenges like speech-in-noise. Inverting this relationship—making a complex sound the target within a tonal background—directly models a key aspect of tinnitus perception, where a persistent internal 'masker' must be detected against a quiet or tonal soundscape. This novel paradigm can probe the mechanisms of auditory object formation and learning in a unique way, with direct relevance to understanding and rehabilitating tinnitus and hidden hearing loss.
proposals.hypothesis: Listeners, including those with normal hearing and those with tinnitus or noise-induced hearing loss, can perceptually learn to detect a spectro-temporally complex masker (e.g., a noise or modulated complex) embedded in a multi-tone background, and this learning will transfer asymmetrically compared to learning in the standard target-in-masker paradigm, revealing distinct underlying neural mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will adapt a frequency discrimination or detection task where the 'target' is a broadband or narrowband noise burst or an amplitude-modulated complex, presented simultaneously with a standard multi-tone complex. Using a 3-interval, 3-alternative forced-choice (3AFC) procedure, participants will indicate which interval contained the target masker. Learning will be tracked over multiple sessions. We will employ pre-2026 deep neural network models (e.g., a convolutional neural network trained on auditory spectrograms) as a quantitative model of the auditory periphery and mid-brain to analyze the differential cue availability (e.g., envelope, fine-structure, spectral profile) in the inverted versus standard paradigm.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new behavioral paradigm for studying auditory perceptual learning and object segregation. It will provide novel data on the plasticity of the auditory system when trained to detect complex objects in simple backgrounds, contrasting with the extensive literature on the reverse. The findings will offer theoretical insights for auditory training strategies aimed at improving the detection of real-world signals in challenging environments and may inform new approaches to tinnitus management by reframing the perceptual task.
proposals.novelty: While decades of research have studied detecting a tone in noise, systematically studying the detection of a 'noise' (or complex masker) in tones is a fundamental inversion of the classic problem. This shift moves beyond modeling external masking to model the internal detection of a complex auditory object, a perspective scarcely explored in pre-2026 psychoacoustics.

proposals.title: Generalization of Perceptual Learning in Tone Detection: A Frequency-Specificity Investigation
proposals.motivation: Perceptual learning in auditory tasks, such as tone detection in noise, is a well-documented phenomenon, but the extent of its generalization across frequencies remains unclear. This is critical for understanding the plasticity of the auditory system and for designing effective auditory training protocols, particularly for individuals with hearing loss or tinnitus, where frequency-specific deficits are common. Determining whether learning is tied to specific cochlear places or to more central, generalized processes has direct implications for rehabilitative strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that perceptual learning in a tone-detection task will show significant frequency specificity, with limited generalization to untrained frequencies, particularly for frequencies distant from the trained frequency. This would suggest learning is strongly influenced by place-specific mechanisms in the early auditory pathway.
proposals.method: We will adapt a classic psychoacoustic paradigm, measuring detection thresholds for a 1-kHz pure tone in a broadband noise masker over multiple training sessions. A control group will train at 1 kHz only. An experimental group will undergo interleaved training at three target frequencies: 0.5 kHz, 1 kHz, and 2 kHz. Thresholds will be tracked using a three-interval, forced-choice adaptive procedure (e.g., a 3-down-1-up rule estimating 79.4% correct). Learning rates and asymptotic performance will be analyzed and compared within and across frequency conditions to assess generalization. Pre- and post-training thresholds will also be measured at several probe frequencies (e.g., 0.25, 0.75, 1.5, 3 kHz) to map the generalization gradient.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a detailed map of the frequency generalization of perceptual learning for a basic auditory detection task. It will clarify the relative roles of place-coding and more central learning mechanisms. The findings will inform models of auditory plasticity and guide the development of targeted auditory training regimens for clinical populations, such as those with noise-induced hearing loss or frequency-specific tinnitus.
proposals.novelty: While learning in frequency discrimination has been studied, systematic investigation of frequency specificity for detection-in-noise learning is less common. This study directly tests the generalization gradient using a rigorous within-subjects design across a range that includes frequencies where temporal (phase-locking) cues are strong (e.g., 0.5 kHz) and weaker (2 kHz), linking the learning effect to underlying coding principles.

proposals.title: Estimating Auditory Perceptual Templates for Frequency Discrimination Using Classification Image Techniques
proposals.motivation: Understanding the internal perceptual templates used in auditory tasks, such as frequency discrimination, is crucial for models of normal and impaired hearing. Current methods for estimating these templates, like the external noise masking paradigm, are indirect and limited. A more direct method would advance our understanding of individual differences in hearing, particularly in listeners with sensorineural hearing loss or tinnitus, where templates may be altered.
proposals.hypothesis: Classification image (CI) techniques, successfully used to reveal perceptual templates in vision, can be adapted to directly estimate the internal weighting functions listeners apply across frequency and time during auditory frequency discrimination. We hypothesize that these auditory CIs will reveal sharper, more focused templates in trained normal-hearing listeners and altered, broader templates in listeners with hearing loss.
proposals.method: We will adapt the psychophysical reverse-correlation framework used in visual CI studies. Listeners will perform a 2AFC frequency discrimination task on a pure-tone target (e.g., 2 kHz) embedded in a multi-tone masker with random frequency components and temporal envelopes. On each trial, the stimulus pattern will be recorded. By reverse-correlating the listener's correct/incorrect decisions with the spectro-temporal pattern of the masker over thousands of trials, we will compute a classification image representing the listener's effective perceptual template. Deep neural network models (e.g., CNNs), pre-2026 state-of-the-art for pattern recognition, will be used to validate and analyze the derived templates across participant groups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, data-driven estimates of auditory perceptual templates, offering a new tool for auditory psychophysics. It will yield quantitative insights into how frequency and temporal information are weighted by listeners with normal and impaired hearing, informing models of auditory processing and potentially guiding the development of more individualized hearing-aid signal processing strategies.
proposals.novelty: While classification images are established in vision science, their application to estimate spectro-temporal perceptual templates in hearing is novel. This approach moves beyond indirect masking methods to visualize the internal weighting functions directly, combining psychophysical techniques with modern data analysis from machine learning.

proposals.title: Auditory Learning for the Simultaneous Detection of Multiple Tones: Investigating Capacity Limits and Perceptual Reorganization in Normal-Hearing and Hearing-Impaired Listeners
proposals.motivation: Auditory perceptual learning is well-studied for single targets, but real-world listening often requires monitoring for multiple concurrent sounds. Pre-2026 work by Moore and others has established detailed models of frequency discrimination and detection for isolated tones, but the mechanisms and limits of learning when listeners must detect two or more simultaneous targets are unknown. This gap is critical for understanding complex listening tasks and for designing better auditory training protocols, especially for individuals with hearing loss or tinnitus who struggle in multi-source environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that learning to detect multiple simultaneous tones involves a fundamental shift from focused, place-based coding to a more distributed, temporal or 'group-based' perceptual strategy, and that this learning will be significantly impaired in listeners with cochlear hearing loss due to reduced frequency selectivity and increased internal noise.
proposals.method: We will employ a longitudinal training paradigm where participants (normal-hearing and hearing-impaired groups) learn to detect two simultaneous target tones (e.g., 1 kHz and 3 kHz) embedded in a broadband noise masker. Targets will be presented at individually calibrated levels near threshold. Performance will be tracked using adaptive procedures (e.g., a 3-interval forced-choice task) to measure detection thresholds for the tone pair over time. We will analyze learning curves and compare them to single-tone baselines. To probe underlying mechanisms, we will use pre- and post-training tests of frequency selectivity (notched-noise masking) and phase-locking sensitivity (based on Moore's 2024 work) to assess changes in peripheral and central processing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic data on the capacity and time course of auditory learning for multiple simultaneous targets. It will directly inform models of auditory attention and perceptual organization. The findings will have practical implications for developing advanced auditory training regimens aimed at improving complex sound perception in hearing aid users and individuals with tinnitus.
proposals.novelty: While previous research has examined multi-tone detection in a static sense, the investigation of *learning* in this multi-target context is novel. Furthermore, linking learning outcomes to individual differences in place and temporal coding (a core theme of Moore's recent work) provides a unique mechanistic perspective not explored in prior learning studies.

proposals.title: Generalization of Auditory Perceptual Learning to Complex Acoustic Scenes in Listeners with Hearing Loss and Tinnitus
proposals.motivation: Auditory training often improves performance in controlled laboratory tasks, but its real-world benefit for listeners with hearing loss or tinnitus remains unclear. This is a critical translational gap, as these individuals struggle most with understanding speech and detecting sounds in noisy, natural environments. Assessing transfer to complex scenes is essential for validating the ecological relevance of perceptual learning paradigms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that frequency discrimination training, which exploits individual differences in place and phase-locking cues, will generalize to improved detection and identification of target sounds within complex, naturalistic auditory scenes, but that the degree of transfer will be modulated by the specific audiometric profile and tinnitus status of the listener.
proposals.method: Participants with varying degrees of high-frequency hearing loss, with and without tinnitus, will complete a baseline adaptive frequency discrimination task at 2 kHz. They will then undergo a multi-session training regimen on this task. Transfer will be assessed using a novel 'auditory scene detection' test, where targets (e.g., specific phonemes, animal calls, or instrument notes) must be identified within realistic background soundscapes (e.g., cafe noise, forest sounds). Performance will be analyzed using deep neural network (DNN) feature representations (e.g., from a pre-trained VGGish or similar audio network) to quantify the acoustic similarity between training and transfer tasks and predict individual learning outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, quantitative test of the real-world applicability of basic auditory training. It will establish a methodological framework for evaluating generalization using ecologically valid stimuli and modern machine-learning analysis. The findings will inform the design of more effective auditory rehabilitation strategies by identifying which listener characteristics predict successful transfer of learning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied frequency discrimination and scene analysis separately, this proposal directly links them by testing causal transfer from a foundational psychoacoustic task to complex scene perception. The integration of individual-difference psychoacoustics, realistic soundscapes, and DNN-based acoustic analysis to model generalization pathways is a novel synthesis of Moore's recent research themes.

proposals.title: Individual Variability in the Transition Frequency: A Data-Driven Challenge to the Fixed 5 kHz Boundary in Auditory Processing
proposals.motivation: Audiological and psychoacoustic models often treat 5 kHz as a fixed boundary, separating perceptual regimes dominated by temporal (phase-locking) cues from those dominated by place (tonotopic) cues. This simplification is foundational for models of frequency discrimination, pitch perception, and hearing loss compensation. However, pre-2026 work by Moore and others on individual differences in frequency discrimination suggests this boundary may not be fixed, potentially limiting the accuracy of hearing-aid algorithms and diagnostic tools that rely on a universal transition point.
proposals.hypothesis: The transition frequency between temporal and place coding dominance is not fixed at 5 kHz but varies systematically across individuals, correlating with factors such as high-frequency hearing loss, age, and cochlear health. This variability can be quantified and predicted using audiological profiles.
proposals.method: We will recruit a large, heterogeneous group of participants with varying degrees of high-frequency hearing loss and age. Using an adaptive 2AFC procedure, we will measure frequency discrimination limens (FDLs) across a wide range (1-8 kHz) to identify the frequency where FDL functions show a characteristic breakpoint, indicating the transition. Deep neural network architectures (specifically, multi-layer perceptron regressors) will be trained on the collected data, using features such as pure-tone audiometric thresholds up to 16 kHz, age, and otoacoustic emission measures, to predict an individual's transition frequency. The model's performance will be validated against held-out data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, empirical map of individual variability in the auditory transition frequency, challenging a key assumption in auditory theory. It will yield a predictive model that can personalize hearing-aid signal processing strategies (e.g., for frequency lowering or noise reduction) and refine diagnostic criteria for noise-induced hearing loss by accounting for individual neural coding limits.
proposals.novelty: While the existence of a transition is well-known, systematically treating its frequency as a variable individual trait and using machine learning to predict it from a standard clinical audiological profile is a novel, data-driven approach that moves beyond group averages.

proposals.title: Ecological Pitch Perception: A Statistical Learning Framework Using Natural Sound Corpora
proposals.motivation: Traditional models of pitch perception, based on isolated tones or synthetic harmonic complexes, may not generalize to real-world listening where pitch is extracted from complex, overlapping sources with natural timbres and noise. This limits the ecological validity of theories and the effectiveness of hearing-aid algorithms designed to aid pitch perception in speech and music. A paradigm shift towards natural sound statistics, akin to the revolution in computer vision, is needed to build robust models of auditory function.
proposals.hypothesis: Computational models trained on the statistical regularities of natural sounds (e.g., speech, music, environmental scenes) will outperform traditional models based on idealized stimuli in predicting human pitch discrimination thresholds and perceptual grouping in complex acoustic mixtures.
proposals.method: We will curate a diverse corpus of natural acoustic scenes with annotated fundamental frequencies (F0s). A deep neural network (DNN) architecture, incorporating known cochlear and midbrain processing stages (e.g., gammatone filtering, neural synchrony simulation), will be trained to estimate pitch salience and track F0 contours from this corpus. Model predictions will be rigorously tested against new psychoacoustic data from normal-hearing and hearing-impaired listeners, using ecologically valid stimuli derived from the corpus, such as competing talkers or music in noise.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, ecologically grounded computational model of pitch perception applicable to complex sounds. It will generate new testable hypotheses about auditory grouping and offer a framework for developing more effective hearing-aid processing strategies, particularly for speech-in-noise and music perception, directly addressing challenges in Professor Moore's recent work on feedback cancellation and frequency discrimination.
proposals.novelty: While natural image statistics are foundational in vision, a systematic application of this principle to model auditory pitch perception from real-world acoustic statistics is novel. It moves beyond testing models with controlled stimuli to building models from the ground up using the statistical structure of the natural auditory environment.

proposals.title: Disentangling Place and Temporal Contributions to the 5 kHz Frequency Discrimination Kink Using Individual Differences and Neural Modeling
proposals.motivation: Auditory frequency discrimination performance shows a distinct degradation near 5 kHz, a phenomenon known as the '5 kHz kink.' This kink is a critical benchmark for auditory theories, as it has been attributed to a shift from temporal to place-based coding mechanisms. However, its precise neural basis remains unresolved, limiting our understanding of fundamental auditory processing and models of hearing loss.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the 5 kHz kink arises from a transition where the precision of phase-locking information becomes insufficient for fine discrimination, forcing increased reliance on less acute place-of-excitation cues. This transition point will vary systematically across individuals based on their high-frequency temporal processing limits.
proposals.method: We will combine psychophysical measures with computational modeling. First, we will measure frequency discrimination limens (FDLs) and temporal fine structure sensitivity across a range (1-8 kHz) in listeners with normal hearing and mild high-frequency loss. Individual differences in these measures will be analyzed to correlate the frequency of the performance kink with independent estimates of temporal processing limits. We will then implement and compare two deep neural network (DNN) architectures: one trained solely on simulated auditory nerve place (rate) information and another incorporating both place and precise temporal (phase-locking) cues up to a model-defined cutoff, to see which best predicts the behavioral kink pattern.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, individual-differences-based test of the dominant theory for the 5 kHz kink. It will clarify the relative contributions of place and temporal coding in mid-to-high-frequency hearing. The findings will inform more physiologically grounded models of hearing impairment and could guide the development of signal processing strategies for hearing aids targeting specific coding deficits.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly linking individual variability in temporal processing capacity to the specific frequency of the behavioral kink, moving beyond population averages. Furthermore, using DNNs as comparative models of different neural coding strategies to explain psychophysical data represents a novel synthesis of Moore's established individual-differences approach with contemporary computational auditory neuroscience methods.

proposals.title: Time-Place Trade-offs in Auditory Discrimination: Testing the Dichotomy with Brief Complex Tones and Noise
proposals.motivation: The classic time-place dichotomy for frequency coding in the auditory system is well-established for long-duration pure tones, but its validity for ecologically relevant, brief, and spectrally complex sounds remains unclear. This gap is critical for understanding the fundamental limits of hearing in noisy environments and for refining models of auditory processing that underpin hearing aid algorithms and tinnitus research. Testing this with complex, short-duration stimuli bridges basic psychoacoustics with real-world auditory challenges.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relative importance of temporal (phase-locking) and place (tonotopic) cues for frequency discrimination will shift for brief, complex stimuli compared to pure tones, with place cues becoming disproportionately dominant for very brief noise bursts, challenging a strict dichotomy.
proposals.method: We will measure difference limens (DLs) for frequency and spectral shape using adaptive 3-interval forced-choice tasks. Stimuli will include brief (e.g., 10-50 ms) harmonic complex tones (missing fundamental), inharmonic complexes, and narrowband noise bursts, presented to normal-hearing and hearing-impaired participants. Following Moore's prior work, we will exploit individual differences in high-frequency hearing loss to infer the contribution of place cues, while manipulating temporal fine structure availability. Deep neural network analysis (similar to Moore's 2025 DNN work on NIHL diagnosis) will be used to model the relationship between individual audiometric profiles and DL patterns across stimulus types.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct test of auditory coding theories for realistic, transient sounds, clarifying the perceptual cues available in everyday listening. It will yield a refined model of frequency representation that can inform the next generation of hearing aid signal processing, particularly for enhancing speech cues in noise, and offer insights into the peripheral constraints relevant to tinnitus generation and perception.
proposals.novelty: While time-place trade-offs have been studied with pure tones, systematically testing the dichotomy with brief complex tones and noise—and linking results to individual hearing profiles via DNN analysis—is a novel synthesis of Moore's core themes. This moves beyond simple stimuli to address the integrated coding of naturalistic sounds.

proposals.title: Modeling Auditory Frequency Discrimination with Signal Detection Theory: A Deep Learning Framework for Individual Differences
proposals.motivation: Understanding the relative contributions of place and temporal (phase-locking) cues to frequency discrimination is a central problem in auditory neuroscience, with direct implications for models of hearing loss and hearing-aid design. Traditional psychophysical methods, as used in Moore's recent work, provide behavioral data but offer limited mechanistic insight into the internal decision process. Integrating classic Signal Detection Theory (SDT) with modern deep learning (DL) offers a powerful, data-driven way to model this internal decision variable and its neural correlates.
proposals.hypothesis: A deep neural network (DNN) trained to perform frequency discrimination tasks can generate an internal representation that, when analyzed through an SDT framework, will accurately model human psychometric functions and reveal distinct decision variable distributions corresponding to reliance on place versus temporal cues.
proposals.method: We will train a convolutional neural network (CNN) on spectrograms of pure-tone pairs (centered around 2 kHz) to perform a 'higher-frequency' discrimination task, mimicking human experiments. The network's final layer pre-activation will serve as the decision variable. We will fit SDT models (d' and criterion) to the network's output distributions for different frequency separations and internal noise levels. This will be validated against existing human behavioral data from listeners with normal hearing and cochlear hearing loss, the latter simulated by degrading specific input representations (e.g., spectral smearing for place code degradation).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a computational bridge between low-level auditory representations and high-level perceptual decisions, formalizing the decision process in frequency discrimination. It will yield a testable, quantitative model that can predict individual performance based on simulated auditory profiles, offering a new tool for diagnosing the specific cue-processing deficits underlying hearing loss.
proposals.novelty: While SDT is a standard psychophysical tool and DL is used in auditory modeling, their direct integration to explicitly model the decision variable for frequency discrimination is novel. This approach moves beyond correlating DL features with behavior to actively using the network's architecture to instantiate and analyze the decision process postulated by SDT.

proposals.title: Data-Driven Characterization of Auditory Filter Dynamics Using Volterra Kernel Identification
proposals.motivation: Current models of the auditory filter, such as the gammatone or roex filters, are typically linear and time-invariant, failing to capture the full complexity of cochlear processing, especially its nonlinear and time-varying properties. A more precise, data-driven characterization of these dynamics is crucial for advancing models of hearing, particularly for understanding phenomena like frequency selectivity in hearing loss, tinnitus generation, and speech perception in noise. This gap limits the accuracy of hearing aid algorithms and diagnostic tools.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the temporal response properties of the human auditory filter can be fully characterized by a low-order Volterra series, and that the identified nonlinear kernels will reveal significant, systematic deviations from linear time-invariant models, particularly in listeners with cochlear hearing loss.
proposals.method: We will adapt the white-noise system identification method from computational neurophysiology. Participants will be presented with a broadband acoustic noise stimulus. The system output will be measured via both physiological methods (e.g., auditory brainstem responses or otoacoustic emissions) and perceptual tasks (e.g., forward masking or frequency discrimination). The input-output relationship will be analyzed using cross-correlation techniques to estimate first-order (linear) and second-order Volterra kernels, providing a complete temporal and nonlinear model of the filter. Analyses will compare kernels across frequency regions and between normal-hearing listeners and those with noise-induced hearing loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, data-driven model of auditory filter dynamics that explicitly quantifies temporal and nonlinear properties. It will offer a new benchmark for auditory modeling, improve the physiological grounding of perceptual phenomena, and inform the development of more accurate signal processing strategies for hearing aids, particularly for feedback cancellation and speech enhancement in complex sounds.
proposals.novelty: While Volterra kernels are established in neurophysiology, their application to characterize the *auditory filter* as a system is novel. This moves beyond fitting static filter shapes to directly identifying the system's dynamic, nonlinear impulse response from empirical data, bridging a gap between physiological system identification and psychophysical auditory models.

ideas.title: Apply Volterra Kernel Identification from Neurophysiology to Characterize Auditory Filter Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational neurophysiology—Volterra kernel identification—to characterize the auditory filter's temporal response. In neurophysiology, this method is used to model complex neural systems by stimulating them with white noise and cross-correlating the output to derive linear and nonlinear response kernels. Adapted to auditory research, we would use a broadband acoustic noise stimulus and measure the system's output via neural recordings or perception. The resulting Volterra kernels would provide a complete, data-driven model of the auditory filter, capturing its precise temporal dynamics and inherent nonlinearities, moving beyond simplified linear time-invariant assumptions.

ideas.title: Model pitch perception using natural sound statistics, not idealized stimuli
ideas.description: This idea challenges the foundational assumption that pitch perception is best studied using isolated pure tones, which are rare in natural listening environments. Drawing an analogy from computer vision, where the shift from controlled images to natural image statistics revolutionized model robustness, we propose a similar paradigm shift for auditory research. The adapted solution is to model pitch perception using the statistical regularities found in natural sounds (like speech harmonics, instrumental timbres, and overlapping environmental pitches). By building experiments and computational models based on a curated corpus of real-world acoustic scenes, we can develop a more accurate and ecologically valid understanding of how the auditory system performs pitch discrimination in everyday life.

ideas.title: A Cross-Modal, Model-Based Test for the Constancy of Perceptual Internal Noise
ideas.description: This research tests the constancy of internal noise by moving beyond single-task sensitivity measures. It employs a computational modeling approach where an individual's perceptual performance is measured across multiple sensory modalities and task structures. The data is fit with ideal observer models that explicitly include internal noise as a free parameter, allowing for statistical comparison between models with a single, global noise parameter and models with modality-specific noise parameters. This design directly addresses the circularity problem by determining if the concept of a unitary internal noise source is sufficient to explain perception, or if it must be considered a variable property of specific neural systems.

ideas.title: Quantifying Dynamic Internal Noise via Neural Cross-Correlation Models in Timing Tasks
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to challenge the assumption of constant internal noise by using computational models of neural cross-correlation or autocorrelation—theorized mechanisms for time measurement—as the investigative tool. By fitting these models to high-precision behavioral and neural data from timing tasks with varying demands (e.g., interval discrimination vs. production), we can derive trial-by-trial estimates of noise parameters within the timing mechanism itself. This allows us to test if internal noise is a fixed property or a dynamic one that scales with task sensitivity, temporal context, or neural resource allocation, thereby unifying the study of noise with the core computational principles of timing.

ideas.title: Applying Quantum Measurement Back-Action to Probe Dynamic Internal Noise
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that internal noise in perceptual or cognitive systems is a constant, intrinsic property. It draws an analogy from quantum measurement physics, where the measurement process inevitably disturbs the system (back-action), confounding the measurement of intrinsic properties. The analogous solution involved developing weak and quantum non-demolition measurement techniques to isolate the system's true state. Adapted to psychophysics, this suggests that standard high-contrast or high-clarity stimuli might act as 'strong' measurements that mask the true dynamics of internal noise. The research would design tasks using near-threshold, ambiguous, or rapidly sequential 'weak probe' stimuli to minimize measurement intrusion. By analyzing how noise estimates change with probe strength and timing—paralleling the study of back-action—we can test whether internal noise is constant or is a dynamic quantity sensitive to the observational context itself.

ideas.title: A Hybrid Place-Time Code: A Unified Oscillatory-Interference Model for All Frequencies
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that different frequency bands in neural oscillations serve segregated functions (e.g., theta for timing/sequence, gamma for place). Drawing an analogy from wireless communication (OFDM), the solution is a unified oscillatory-interference model where spatial and temporal information is multiplexed across all frequencies simultaneously. In this adapted model, a population's activity is defined by a bank of continuous oscillators. The precise representation of location or cognitive state is given by a composite code: the instantaneous phase relationship (a temporal cue) of each oscillator and its specific spectral identity or 'channel' (a place cue). This hybrid place-time mechanism operates at all frequencies, allowing for robust, high-capacity, and noise-resistant neural representation, much like OFDM allows robust data transmission across noisy channels by distributing information across multiple spectral bins.

ideas.title: Comparative phylogenetic analysis of neural phase-locking to distinguish universal biophysical limits from lineage-specific adaptations
ideas.description: This redesigned study tests the proposed 5 kHz boundary by measuring neural phase-locking limits not in isolated animals, but across a strategically selected phylogenetic spectrum of vertebrates (e.g., mammals without high-frequency specialization, birds, anurans). The core innovation is the analytical framework that separates a potential fundamental biophysical constraint—evidenced by a conserved upper limit across independent evolutionary lineages—from lineage-specific adaptations evidenced by limits that correlate with ecological auditory specializations. This approach directly tests whether an observed boundary is a universal neural computation limit or merely a species-specific trait.

ideas.title: Dissociating Perceptual Learning from Attentional Selection via Drift Diffusion Modeling in a Dual-Task Paradigm
ideas.description: This research tests the hypothesis that perceptual learning can operate through changes in the rate of sensory evidence accumulation (drift rate), a low-level perceptual factor distinct from selective attention. Using a dual-task paradigm where a primary orientation discrimination task is paired with a secondary auditory detection task, we apply hierarchical drift diffusion modeling to behavioral data. This allows us to quantify trial-by-trial changes in drift rate, decision boundary, and non-decision time, isolating improvements in sensory processing efficiency from shifts in attentional allocation or response caution. A key prediction is that learning on the primary task will manifest as an increased drift rate without proportional cost to secondary task performance, providing direct evidence for a non-attentional learning mechanism.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Information Capacity of Neural Weight Changes During Learning
ideas.description: This research direction integrates behavioral reverse-correlation, which reveals the perceptual or cognitive 'weight vectors' a subject learns, with neural recordings (EEG/fMRI) and formal information theory. The core innovation is to treat the evolving neural representations of these weight vectors as a communication channel, calculating the information rate (in bits per unit time or per trial) at which task-relevant features are encoded and updated in the brain. This allows us to move from asking 'where' and 'when' weight changes correlate with neural activity to asking 'how much' information these neural plasticity mechanisms can transmit, directly testing hypotheses about the channel capacity and efficiency of different brain systems during learning.

ideas.title: Apply medical diagnostic ROC analysis to DL decision confidence
ideas.description: This research adapts the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) framework from medical diagnostics—where it separates diagnostic test sensitivity from physician decision bias—to analyze deep learning models in frequency discrimination tasks. In medical diagnostics, ROC curves plot true positive rate against false positive rate across decision thresholds, quantifying both discriminative power (d') and response criterion independently. By treating correct frequency discrimination as 'signal' and incorrect discrimination as 'noise,' we can apply this same analytical framework to DL models. This allows us to generate ROC curves for different model architectures and training regimes, separating their true frequency discrimination sensitivity from their internal decision confidence thresholds. The approach provides a more nuanced evaluation than simple accuracy metrics, revealing whether poor performance stems from inadequate feature discrimination or from suboptimal decision criteria, with applications for model optimization and interpretability.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Signal Cross-Correlation for Neural Time Measurement
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics to the computational neuroscience context of time measurement. In seismology, a method called ambient noise cross-correlation is used to extract precise wave travel times between seismic stations from long recordings of background seismic noise, which is typically considered useless signal. The analogous problem in neuroscience is measuring the brain's internal timing mechanisms from ongoing, variable neural activity. The adapted research idea proposes to model the time-measuring mechanism by using neural network architectures designed to perform continuous cross-correlation on streams of neural data (such as local field potentials or multi-unit activity). The network would be trained to detect and quantify consistent temporal intervals or propagation latencies embedded within the brain's endogenous 'noisy' signals, thereby revealing the neural correlates of time perception and measurement without relying on external rhythmic stimuli.

ideas.title: Dynamic Hierarchical Signal Detection Theory for Characterizing Evolving Decision Processes in Deep Networks
ideas.description: This research develops a dynamic, hierarchical Bayesian extension of signal detection theory (SDT) specifically designed for deep learning systems performing discrimination tasks. The method estimates trial-by-trial internal signal and noise distributions directly from the network's latent representations across different layers and training epochs, rather than assuming static psychometric parameters. By modeling the decision criterion and perceptual sensitivity (d') as functions of the network's evolving feature space, it captures how learning alters both representation quality and decision strategy. This approach provides a robust, interpretable framework for comparing perceptual decision-making across architectures, training regimes, and biological systems.

ideas.title: Contingent Spectral Learning: Embedding Task-Relevant Information within Predictable Spectral Scaffolds
ideas.description: This approach designs stimuli where a predictable, repeating spectral pattern (the 'scaffold') is not a masker but the essential medium for the task. The target information (e.g., a classification label or detection cue) is encoded through specific, learnable modifications to this scaffold, such as a consistent spectral notch, a predictable amplitude modulation of a particular band, or a phase shift at a known frequency. The model must therefore learn the predictable baseline pattern to reliably detect the contingent, information-carrying deviation. This directly tests if and how the learning strategy adapts to extract information from spectral structures, moving beyond mere exposure to predictability.

ideas.title: Context-Gated Hybrid Coding: A Dynamic Model for Cue Integration
ideas.description: This model proposes that the brain uses a flexible, context-dependent gating mechanism to integrate place and time cues, rather than a rigid, always-on hybrid. The system continuously monitors the reliability of available spatial and temporal signals, as well as task demands like planning versus recall, to dynamically adjust the weighting of each cue type. This allows for robust performance when one cue source is degraded or irrelevant, and efficient computation by focusing resources on the most informative signals. The result is a unified but adaptive framework that explains how a hybrid system can be both powerful and biologically plausible.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Drift-Robust Time Measurement via Meta-Learned Correlation and Online Noise Estimation
ideas.description: This system addresses the fragility of pure neural correlation to signal non-stationarity by creating a closed-loop measurement architecture. A core cross-correlation module provides initial timing estimates, which are fed into an adaptive Kalman filter that explicitly models and compensates for dynamic signal drift and noise. A meta-learning controller periodically updates the correlation model using data synthesized to match the currently observed environmental noise profile, ensuring the front-end remains optimized for real-world conditions. This results in a robust time-measuring mechanism that maintains accuracy despite changing signal characteristics.

ideas.title: Adapting Geophysical Tomography to Map Neural Weight Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from geophysical seismic tomography to the problem of linking behavioral reverse-correlation weight vectors to neural correlates. In the analogy, the behavioral weights derived from learning trials are treated as the sources of a signal, analogous to earthquakes, while the multi-channel EEG or voxel-wise fMRI data are the distributed receiver measurements. The analogous solution—solving an inverse problem to reconstruct a hidden spatial model from surface measurements—is adapted by developing a computational inversion framework. This framework would estimate the latent neural representational space or dynamic network whose activity patterns best predict the observed correlation between the evolving behavioral weight vectors and the concurrent neural signals, thereby providing a tomographic 'map' of the neural substrate of learned perceptual or decision weights.

ideas.title: Temporal Dominance of Non-Attentional Perceptual Learning
ideas.description: This research investigates the hypothesis that the initial, brief dominance of place-based neural mechanisms in learning is primarily driven by rapid, non-attentional perceptual processes, such as feature binding or sensory adaptation. It posits that the subsequent requirement for longer durations for time-based mechanisms, like memory consolidation, reflects a shift to cognitive processes that integrate these initial perceptual signatures. The core innovation is experimentally decoupling attentional load from perceptual input to test if the place-time inversion effect persists, thereby revealing if the foundational layer of learning is a fast, pre-attentive perceptual construction. This moves beyond asking 'if' non-attentional factors matter to defining 'when' and 'how' they govern the temporal hierarchy of learning mechanisms.

ideas.title: Information-Theoretic Capacity of a Spatiotemporal Communication Channel with Model Validation
ideas.description: This research first formally defines a spatiotemporal communication channel, specifying how information is encoded into discrete place-time symbols, the physical constraints on symbol generation and detection, and the dominant noise processes. Using this formal model, it calculates the theoretical channel capacity by applying information theory to the derived probabilistic channel matrix. Finally, the theoretical capacity is validated and its practical implications are explored through a simulation framework that models symbol transmission, noise corruption, and optimal decoding, ensuring the results are robust and physically meaningful.

ideas.title: Assume learning is not purely attentional: An ecological perception analogy
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that learning is purely attentional by drawing an analogy from ecological psychology's solution to perceptual learning. In that field, learning was reconceptualized not as building internal representations from attended features, but as the organism's attunement to invariant, directly perceivable structures in the environment (affordances). Adapting this solution, the research would investigate if cognitive and perceptual learning effects can be driven by factors beyond selective attention, specifically by the passive pickup of latent statistical or structural regularities (e.g., global patterns, cross-modal synchronicities) in the sensory stream. The experiment would test whether robust learning occurs for task-irrelevant environmental invariants, thereby implicating a fundamental perceptual mechanism for information integration that operates in parallel with, or as a foundation for, attentional processes.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Succession Dynamics to Neural Mechanisms
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from ecological succession, where different biological strategies dominate at different temporal scales, to challenge the assumption about place and time mechanisms in neural systems. In ecology, r-selected pioneer species rapidly colonize and dominate a space immediately after a disturbance, analogous to place mechanisms acting briefly. Over longer durations, K-selected climax species gradually take over through competitive efficiency and stability, analogous to time mechanisms requiring longer durations to manifest dominance. The adapted research idea applies this framework to hypothesize that in neural computation, fast, spatially constrained 'place' mechanisms (like specific synaptic connections or local circuit motifs) dominate the initial phase of information processing or learning. In contrast, slower, temporally extended 'time' mechanisms (like rate coding, plasticity rules, or network-wide oscillations) require longer durations to exert their dominant influence and stabilize the system's state. The experimental approach would involve designing interventions that selectively target spatial vs. temporal aspects of neural activity at different time points following a stimulus or during a learning task, measuring which intervention disrupts function more at brief vs. extended timescales.

ideas.title: Assess the stability and specificity of long-term attentional control memory
ideas.description: This study uses a longitudinal, multi-group design to dissect true retention of learned attention from non-specific performance changes. Participants undergo initial attention training, followed by a series of assessments over weeks that include standard probes, performance under cognitive load, and a critical abbreviated 'reinstatement' training session to measure savings in re-learning. A matched active control group, trained on a different but equally demanding cognitive task, controls for practice effects and placebo. The primary evidence for retention is a significant interaction, where the trained group shows faster re-learning and greater robustness to interference after the delay compared to controls, indicating a persistent, specific memory trace for attentional control.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Temporal Dynamics of Place and Time Code Dominance in Memory Systems
ideas.description: This research program aims to empirically map how neural representations relying on spatial context ('place') and sequential structure ('time') vary in their relative influence over different experience durations. We will record population activity from hippocampal and entorhinal circuits in rodents performing tasks with systematically varied epoch lengths. Using a unified analytical framework, we will quantify the contribution of each coding scheme and model the continuous function describing their interaction across timescales, moving beyond a simple binary inversion to reveal the governing principles of their dynamic interplay.

ideas.title: Applying Thermodynamic Entropy Constraints to Spatiotemporal Channel Capacity
ideas.description: This research seed proposes applying information theory to calculate the channel capacity of proposed place and time mechanisms. Through analogical reasoning with thermodynamics, we adapt the solution of using free energy to bound useful work. The analogous field is Thermodynamics and Statistical Mechanics, where the maximum useful work is constrained by free energy (total energy minus temperature*entropy). The adapted idea is to treat the spatiotemporal channel as a thermodynamic system. We define a 'spatiotemporal free capacity' as the total theoretical channel capacity minus a penalty term proportional to the entropy of the spatiotemporal constraints (e.g., positional or synchronization uncertainty). This framework provides a method to calculate the net, practically achievable information rate of the mechanism, formally separating the ideal capacity from the cost imposed by the inherent disorder in its place and time parameters.

ideas.title: Assessing the Decay Rate of Learned Attentional Control Using Ecological Momentary Assessment
ideas.description: This idea adapts the method from immunology for tracking long-term immune memory to measure the retention of learned attention. Just as vaccinologists measure antibody titers over time to plot the decay of immune protection, this study would administer a focused attention-training protocol and then track performance on standardized attention probes at multiple, spaced intervals (e.g., 1 day, 1 week, 1 month, 3 months) without any intervening practice or booster training. The probes, delivered via Ecological Momentary Assessment on smartphones to capture performance in real-world contexts, will allow researchers to model the decay curve of attentional control, quantifying how well the improved selective attention is retained over the long term.
